T1573.001.name: Symmetric Cryptography
T1573.001.description: Adversaries may employ a known symmetric encryption algorithm to conceal command and control traffic rather than relying on any inherent protections provided by a communication protocol. Symmetric encryption algorithms use the same key for plaintext encryption and ciphertext decryption. Common symmetric encryption algorithms include AES, DES, 3DES, Blowfish, and RC4.
T1573.001.mitigations: Mitigation 'Network Intrusion Prevention': Network intrusion detection and prevention systems that use network signatures to identify traffic for specific adversary malware can be used to mitigate activity at the network level.
T1573.001.detections:
Detection Strategy: Detection Strategy for Encrypted Channel via Symmetric Cryptography across OS Platforms
- Analytic 'Analytic 0400': Processes that typically do not perform cryptographic operations loading symmetric encryption libraries (e.g., bcryptprimitives.dll, aes.dll), then initiating outbound connections with high-entropy payloads. Defender correlates process creation, DLL load, and anomalous encrypted traffic patterns.
- Analytic 'Analytic 0401': Unexpected processes (e.g., bash, python, custom binaries) dynamically loading libcrypto or performing AES/RC4 encryption operations, then initiating outbound sessions with abnormal byte entropy or asymmetric traffic patterns.
- Analytic 'Analytic 0402': Launchd jobs or user processes invoking symmetric crypto APIs from the Security framework and generating outbound connections carrying randomized payloads inconsistent with normal TLS patterns.
- Analytic 'Analytic 0403': ESXi daemons (hostd, vpxa) unexpectedly using symmetric encryption routines for external connections. Defender identifies logs of service traffic with encrypted payloads inconsistent with VMware management baselines.
- Analytic 'Analytic 0404': Flows showing encrypted payloads with high entropy not matching TLS handshake patterns, particularly when occurring on non-standard ports. Defender observes NetFlow/IPFIX byte distribution anomalies or IDS/IPS detecting symmetric encryption patterns without associated key exchange.
T1573.001.procedures: Software 'QuasarRAT': QuasarRAT uses AES with a hardcoded pre-shared key to encrypt network communication. Software 'FRP': FRP can use STCP (Secret TCP) with a preshared key to encrypt services exposed to public networks. Software 'Sliver': Sliver can use AES-GCM-256 to encrypt a session key for C2 message exchange. Software 'Dridex': Dridex has encrypted traffic with RC4. Software 'SMOKEDHAM': SMOKEDHAM has encrypted its C2 traffic with RC4. Software 'InvisiMole': InvisiMole uses variations of a simple XOR encryption routine for C&C communications. Software 'StarProxy': StarProxy has leveraged two 256-byte XOR keys to encrypt and decrypt network packets using a custom algorithm. Software 'SysUpdate': SysUpdate has used DES to encrypt all C2 communications. Software 'Emotet': Emotet is known to use RSA keys for encrypting C2 traffic. Software 'Prikormka': Prikormka encrypts some C2 traffic with the Blowfish cipher. Software '3PARA RAT': 3PARA RAT command and control commands are encrypted within the HTTP C2 channel using the DES algorithm in CBC mode with a key derived from the MD5 hash of the string HYF54&%9&jkMCXuiS. 3PARA RAT will use an 8-byte XOR key derived from the string HYF54&%9&jkMCXuiS if the DES decoding fails Software 'LockBit 3.0': LockBit 3.0 can encrypt C2 communications with AES. Software 'NETEAGLE': NETEAGLE will decrypt resources it downloads with HTTP requests by using RC4 with the key "ScoutEagle." Software 'Machete': Machete has used AES to exfiltrate documents. Software 'Azorult': Azorult can encrypt C2 traffic using XOR. Software 'Bisonal': Bisonal variants reported on in 2014 and 2015 used a simple XOR cipher for C2. Some Bisonal samples encrypt C2 communications with RC4. Software 'Sys10': Sys10 uses an XOR 0x1 loop to encrypt its C2 domain. Software 'Elise': Elise encrypts exfiltrated data with RC4. Software 'Winnti for Windows': Winnti for Windows can XOR encrypt C2 traffic. Software 'Pandora': Pandora has the ability to encrypt communications with D3DES. Software 'BADNEWS': BADNEWS encrypts C2 data with a ROR by 3 and an XOR by 0x23. Software 'NDiskMonitor': NDiskMonitor uses AES to encrypt certain information sent over its C2 channel. Software 'QUIETCANARY': QUIETCANARY can RC4 encrypt C2 communications. Software 'Sardonic': Sardonic has the ability to use an RC4 key to encrypt communications to and from actor-controlled C2 servers. Software 'Attor': Attor has encrypted data symmetrically using a randomly generated Blowfish (OFB) key which is encrypted with a public RSA key. Software 'Lurid': Lurid performs XOR encryption. Software 'StrifeWater': StrifeWater can encrypt C2 traffic using XOR with a hard coded key. Software 'Chaos': Chaos provides a reverse shell connection on 8338/TCP, encrypted via AES. Software 'POWERTON': POWERTON has used AES for encrypting C2 traffic. Software 'Havoc': Havoc can send an AES encrypted check-in request to the C2 server. Software 'SUNBURST': SUNBURST encrypted C2 traffic using a single-byte-XOR cipher. Software 'More_eggs': More_eggs has used an RC4-based encryption method for its C2 communications. Software 'Hikit': Hikit performs XOR encryption. Software 'NETWIRE': NETWIRE can use AES encryption for C2 data transferred. Software 'Helminth': Helminth encrypts data sent to its C2 server over HTTP with RC4. Software 'LODEINFO': LODEINFO can encrypt C2 communication with a hardcoded (NV4HDOeOVyL) Vigenere cipher key. Software 'HotCroissant': HotCroissant has compressed network communications and encrypted them with a custom stream cipher. Software 'VIRTUALPIE': VIRTUALPIE can use a custom RC4 encrypted protocol for C2 communications. Software 'HTTPTroy': HTTPTroy has obfuscated request communications utilizing XOR encryption. Software 'Bonadan': Bonadan can XOR-encrypt C2 communications. Software 'Lucifer': Lucifer can perform a decremental-xor encryption on the initial C2 request before sending it over the wire. Software 'Taidoor': Taidoor uses RC4 to encrypt the message body of HTTP content. Software 'Hi-Zor': Hi-Zor encrypts C2 traffic with a double XOR using two distinct single-byte keys. Software 'FALLCHILL': FALLCHILL encrypts C2 data with RC4 encryption. Software 'Mosquito': Mosquito uses a custom encryption algorithm, which consists of XOR and a stream that is similar to the Blum Blum Shub algorithm. Software 'Dipsind': Dipsind encrypts C2 data with AES256 in ECB mode. Software 'MuddyViper': MuddyViper has the ability to encrypt C2 communication using AES-CBC using the CNG API, the key `0608101047106453101617106423101013101012101083109710108585106969`, and the initialization vector `0`. Software 'CharmPower': CharmPower can send additional modules over C2 encrypted with a simple substitution cipher. Software 'BADCALL': BADCALL encrypts C2 traffic using an XOR/ADD cipher. Software 'WarzoneRAT': WarzoneRAT can encrypt its C2 with RC4 with the password `warzone160\x00`. Software 'HAMMERTOSS': Before being appended to image files, HAMMERTOSS commands are encrypted with a key composed of both a hard-coded value and a string contained on that day's tweet. To decrypt the commands, an investigator would need access to the intended malware sample, the day's tweet, and the image file containing the command. Software 'Mango': Mango can receive XOR-encrypted commands from C2. Software 'LunarWeb': LunarWeb can send AES encrypted C2 commands. Software 'RIFLESPINE': RIFLESPINE can use the AES algorithm to encrypt C2 data. Software 'RustyWater': RustyWater has encrypted encoded data with XOR before sending it to the C2 server. Software 'Uroburos': Uroburos can encrypt the data beneath its http2 or tcp encryption at the session layer with CAST-128, using a different key for incoming and outgoing data. Software 'SideTwist': SideTwist can encrypt C2 communications with a randomly generated key. Software 'OSX_OCEANLOTUS.D': OSX_OCEANLOTUS.D encrypts data sent back to the C2 using AES in CBC mode with a null initialization vector (IV) and a key sent from the server that is padded to 32 bytes. Software 'Emissary': The C2 server response to a beacon sent by a variant of Emissary contains a 36-character GUID value that is used as an encryption key for subsequent network communications. Some variants of Emissary use various XOR operations to encrypt C2 data. Software 'FakeM': The original variant of FakeM encrypts C2 traffic using a custom encryption cipher that uses an XOR key of “YHCRA” and bit rotation between each XOR operation. Some variants of FakeM use RC4 to encrypt C2 traffic. Software 'QakBot': QakBot can RC4 encrypt strings in C2 communication. Software 'Mongall': Mongall has the ability to RC4 encrypt C2 communications. Software 'Cardinal RAT': Cardinal RAT uses a secret key with a series of XOR and addition operations to encrypt C2 traffic. Software 'Sakula': Sakula encodes C2 traffic with single-byte XOR keys. Software 'TAMECAT': TAMECAT has used AES to encrypt C2 traffic. Software 'Volgmer': Volgmer uses a simple XOR cipher to encrypt traffic and files. Software 'httpclient': httpclient encrypts C2 content with XOR using a single byte, 0x12. Software 'LIGHTWIRE': LIGHTWIRE can RC4 encrypt C2 commands. Software 'HiddenWasp': HiddenWasp uses an RC4-like algorithm with an already computed PRGA generated key-stream for network communication. Software 'MoonWind': MoonWind encrypts C2 traffic using RC4 with a static key. Software 'Ninja': Ninja can XOR and AES encrypt C2 messages. Software 'Pikabot': Earlier Pikabot variants use a custom encryption procedure leveraging multiple mechanisms including AES with multiple rounds of Base64 encoding for its command and control communication. Later Pikabot variants eliminate the use of AES and instead use RC4 encryption for transmitted information. Software 'GrimAgent': GrimAgent can use an AES key to encrypt C2 communications. Software 'UPPERCUT': Some versions of UPPERCUT have used the hard-coded string “this is the encrypt key” for Blowfish encryption when communicating with a C2. Later versions have hard-coded keys uniquely for each C2 address. UPPERCUT has also used custom ChaCha20, XOR, and LZO algorithms for C2 communication. Software '4H RAT': 4H RAT obfuscates C2 communication using a 1-byte XOR with the key 0xBE. Software 'Winnti for Linux': Winnti for Linux has used a custom TCP protocol with four-byte XOR for command and control (C2). Software 'PUBLOAD': PUBLOAD has used RC4 encryption in C2 communications. Software 'THINCRUST': THINCRUST can process RSA encryted C2 commands. Software 'TrickBot': TrickBot uses a custom crypter leveraging Microsoft’s CryptoAPI to encrypt C2 traffic.Newer versions of TrickBot have been known to use `bcrypt` to encrypt and digitally sign responses to their C2 server. Software 'Stuxnet': Stuxnet encodes the payload of system information sent to the command and control servers using a one byte 0xFF XOR key. Stuxnet also uses a 31-byte long static byte string to XOR data sent to command and control servers. The servers use a different static key to encrypt replies to the implant. Software 'Torisma': Torisma has encrypted its C2 communications using XOR and VEST-32. Software 'Ebury': Ebury has encrypted C2 traffic using the client IP address, then encoded it as a hexadecimal string. Software 'RDAT': RDAT has used AES ciphertext to encode C2 communications. Software 'XCSSET': XCSSET uses RC4 encryption over TCP to communicate with its C2 server. Software 'Samurai': Samurai can encrypt C2 communications with AES. Software 'ZIPLINE': ZIPLINE can use AES-128-CBC to encrypt data for both upload and download. Software 'Bazar': Bazar can send C2 communications with XOR encryption. Software 'Duqu': The Duqu command and control protocol's data stream can be encrypted with AES-CBC. Software 'Rifdoor': Rifdoor has encrypted command and control (C2) communications with a stream cipher. Software 'Hydraq': Hydraq C2 traffic is encrypted using bitwise NOT and XOR operations. Software 'BBSRAT': BBSRAT uses a custom encryption algorithm on data sent back to the C2 server over HTTP. Software 'ADVSTORESHELL': A variant of ADVSTORESHELL encrypts some C2 with 3DES. Software 'TONESHELL': TONESHELL has used RC4 encryption in C2 communications. TONESHELL variants used a randomly generated variable length (0x20 - 0x200 bytes) rolling XOR key to encrypt and decrypt network packets. Software 'LightNeuron': LightNeuron uses AES to encrypt C2 traffic. Software 'Explosive': Explosive has encrypted communications with the RC4 method. Software 'RTM': RTM encrypts C2 traffic with a custom RC4 variant. Software 'Solar': Solar can XOR encrypt C2 communications. Software 'LookBack': LookBack uses a modified version of RC4 for data transfer. Software 'TAINTEDSCRIBE': TAINTEDSCRIBE uses a Linear Feedback Shift Register (LFSR) algorithm for network encryption. Software 'Kobalos': Kobalos's post-authentication communication channel uses a 32-byte-long password with RC4 for inbound and outbound traffic. Software 'gh0st RAT': gh0st RAT uses RC4 and XOR to encrypt C2 traffic. Software 'CASTLETAP': CASTLETAP can receive a 9-byte XOR encrypted activation string in the payload of an ICMP echo request packet. Software 'Mafalda': Mafalda can encrypt its C2 traffic with RC4. Software 'SombRAT': SombRAT has encrypted its C2 communications with AES. Software 'WellMess': WellMess can encrypt HTTP POST data using RC6 and a dynamically generated AES key encrypted with a hard coded RSA public key. Software 'SystemBC': SystemBC has encrypted its C2 traffic with RC4. Software 'Downdelph': Downdelph uses RC4 to encrypt C2 responses. Software 'BOOKWORM': BOOKWORM has used encryption and compression algorithms to obfuscate the traffic between the system and C2 server, methods observed included RC4, AES, XOR with 0x5a, and LZO. Software 'Comnie': Comnie encrypts command and control communications with RC4. Software 'BendyBear': BendyBear communicates to a C2 server over port 443 using modified RC4 and XOR-encrypted chunks. Software 'PipeMon': PipeMon communications are RC4 encrypted. Software 'HyperStack': HyperStack has used RSA encryption for C2 communications. Software 'Gazer': Gazer uses custom encryption for C2 that uses 3DES. Software 'NGLite': NGLite will use an AES encrypted channel for command and control purposes, in one case using the key WHATswrongwithUu. Software 'NanoCore': NanoCore uses DES to encrypt the C2 traffic. Software 'Derusbi': Derusbi obfuscates C2 traffic with variable 4-byte XOR keys. Software 'SLIGHTPULSE': SLIGHTPULSE can RC4 encrypt all incoming and outgoing C2 messages. Software 'Carbanak': Carbanak encrypts the message body of HTTP traffic with RC2 (in CBC mode). Carbanak also uses XOR with random keys for its communications. Software 'xCaon': xCaon has encrypted data sent to the C2 server using a XOR key. Software 'Okrum': Okrum uses AES to encrypt network traffic. The key can be hardcoded or negotiated with the C2 server in the registration phase. Software 'PoisonIvy': PoisonIvy uses the Camellia cipher to encrypt communications. Software 'Bumblebee': Bumblebee can encrypt C2 requests and responses with RC4 Software 'GreyEnergy': GreyEnergy encrypts communications using AES256. Software 'SodaMaster': SodaMaster can use RC4 to encrypt C2 communications. Software 'RedLeaves': RedLeaves has encrypted C2 traffic with RC4, previously using keys of 88888888 and babybear. Software 'PureCrypter': PureCrypter can use AES to encrypt system information sent to the C2. Software 'FlawedAmmyy': FlawedAmmyy has used SEAL encryption during the initial C2 handshake. Software 'Epic': Epic encrypts commands from the C2 server using a hardcoded key. Software 'Latrodectus': Latrodectus can send RC4 encrypted data over C2 channels. Software 'CallMe': CallMe uses AES to encrypt C2 traffic. Software 'TSCookie': TSCookie has encrypted network communications with RC4. Software 'Komplex': The Komplex C2 channel uses an 11-byte XOR algorithm to hide data. Software 'PlugX': PlugX can use RC4 encryption in C2 communications. Software 'WIREFIRE': WIREFIRE can AES encrypt process output sent from compromised devices to C2. Software 'Cobalt Strike': Cobalt Strike has the ability to use AES-256 symmetric encryption in CBC mode with HMAC-SHA-256 to encrypt task commands and XOR to encrypt shell code and configuration data. Software 'SeaDuke': SeaDuke C2 traffic has been encrypted with RC4 and AES. Software 'RIPTIDE': APT12 has used the RIPTIDE RAT, which communicates over HTTP with a payload encrypted with RC4. Software 'H1N1': H1N1 encrypts C2 traffic using an RC4 key. Software 'KONNI': KONNI has used AES to encrypt C2 traffic. Software 'Daserf': Daserf uses RC4 encryption to obfuscate HTTP traffic. Software 'ZeroT': ZeroT has used RC4 to encrypt C2 traffic. Software 'HiddenFace': HiddenFace can use a randomly selected symmetric encryption algorithm for C2. Software 'Troll Stealer': Troll Stealer encrypts data sent to command and control infrastructure using a combination of RC4 and RSA-4096 algorithms. Software 'FatDuke': FatDuke can AES encrypt C2 communications. Software 'KEYMARBLE': KEYMARBLE uses a customized XOR algorithm to encrypt C2 communications. Software 'Bandook': Bandook has used AES encryption for C2 communication. Software 'IceApple': The IceApple Result Retriever module can AES encrypt C2 responses. Software 'RainyDay': RainyDay can use RC4 to encrypt C2 communications. Software 'PLAINTEE': PLAINTEE encodes C2 beacons using XOR. Software 'FoggyWeb': FoggyWeb has used a dynamic XOR key and custom XOR methodology for C2 communications. Software 'BLINDINGCAN': BLINDINGCAN has encrypted its C2 traffic with RC4. Software 'ChChes': ChChes can encrypt C2 traffic with AES or RC4. Software 'metaMain': metaMain can encrypt the data that it sends and receives from the C2 server using an RC4 encryption algorithm. Software 'UBoatRAT': UBoatRAT encrypts instructions in its C2 network payloads using a simple XOR cipher. Software 'Metamorfo': Metamorfo has encrypted C2 commands with AES-256. Software 'SNUGRIDE': SNUGRIDE encrypts C2 traffic using AES with a static key. Software 'PLEAD': PLEAD has used RC4 encryption to download modules. Software 'CHOPSTICK': CHOPSTICK encrypts C2 communications with RC4. Software 'CORESHELL': CORESHELL C2 messages are encrypted with custom stream ciphers using six-byte or eight-byte keys. Software 'RotaJakiro': RotaJakiro encrypts C2 communication using a combination of AES, XOR, ROTATE encryption, and ZLIB compression. Software 'down_new': down_new has the ability to AES encrypt C2 communications. Software 'Felismus': Some Felismus samples use a custom encryption method for C2 traffic that utilizes AES and multiple keys. Software 'Nebulae': Nebulae can use RC4 and XOR to encrypt C2 communications. Software 'Woody RAT': Woody RAT can use AES-CBC to encrypt data sent to its C2 server. Software 'CosmicDuke': CosmicDuke contains a custom version of the RC4 algorithm that includes a programming error. Software 'PingPull': PingPull can use AES, in cipher block chaining (CBC) mode padded with PKCS5, to encrypt C2 server communications. Group 'Mustang Panda': Mustang Panda has encrypted C2 communications with RC4. Mustang Panda has also leveraged encryption and compression algorithms to obfuscate the traffic between the system and C2 server, methods observed included RC4, AES, XOR with 0x5a, and LZO. Group 'Darkhotel': Darkhotel has used AES-256 and 3DES for C2 communications. Group 'Lazarus Group': Several Lazarus Group malware families encrypt C2 traffic using custom code that uses XOR with an ADD operation and XOR with a SUB operation. Another Lazarus Group malware sample XORs C2 traffic. Other Lazarus Group malware uses Caracachs encryption to encrypt C2 payloads. Lazarus Group has also used AES to encrypt C2 traffic.During Operation Dream Job, Lazarus Group used an AES key to communicate with their C2 server. Group 'ZIRCONIUM': ZIRCONIUM has used AES encrypted communications in C2. Group 'Volt Typhoon': Volt Typhoon has used a version of the Awen web shell that employed AES encryption and decryption for C2 communications. Group 'RedCurl': RedCurl has used AES-128 CBC to encrypt C2 communications. Group 'Stealth Falcon': Stealth Falcon malware encrypts C2 traffic using RC4 with a hard-coded key. Group 'MuddyWater': MuddyWater has used AES to encrypt C2 responses. Group 'Contagious Interview': Contagious Interview has encrypted C2 traffic using RC4. Group 'Higaisa': Higaisa used AES-128 to encrypt C2 traffic. Group 'Inception': Inception has encrypted network communications with AES. Group 'APT33': APT33 has used AES for encryption of command and control traffic. Group 'APT28': APT28 installed a Delphi backdoor that used a custom algorithm for C2 communications. Group 'BRONZE BUTLER': BRONZE BUTLER has used RC4 encryption (for Datper malware) and AES (for xxmm malware) to obfuscate HTTP traffic. BRONZE BUTLER has also used a tool called RarStar that encodes data with a custom XOR algorithm when posting it to a C2 server. Group 'UNC3886': In mid-2024 Mandiant identified custom TINYSHELL-based backdoors deployed on Juniper Networks’ Junos OS routers. Mandiant attributed these backdoors to the China-nexus espionage group UNC3886.During RedPenguin, UNC3886 malware used the RC4 cipher to encrypt outgoing C2 messages. Group 'AppleJeus': During the 3CX Supply Chain Attack, AppleJeus's VEILEDSIGNAL communication module supports three commands to conduct the following actions: send implant data, execute shellcode, and terminate itself. Campaign 'Operation Dream Job': During Operation Dream Job, Lazarus Group used an AES key to communicate with their C2 server. Campaign 'RedPenguin': During RedPenguin, UNC3886 malware used the RC4 cipher to encrypt outgoing C2 messages. Campaign '3CX Supply Chain Attack': During the 3CX Supply Chain Attack, AppleJeus's VEILEDSIGNAL communication module supports three commands to conduct the following actions: send implant data, execute shellcode, and terminate itself. Campaign 'Frankenstein': During Frankenstein, the threat actors communicated with C2 via an encrypted RC4 byte stream and AES-CBC.

T1027.013.name: Encrypted/Encoded File
T1027.013.description:
Adversaries may encrypt or encode files to obfuscate strings, bytes, and other specific patterns to impede detection. Encrypting and/or encoding file content aims to conceal malicious artifacts within a file used in an intrusion. Many other techniques, such as [Software Packing](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1027/002), [Steganography](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1027/003), and [Embedded Payloads](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1027/009), share this same broad objective. Encrypting and/or encoding files could lead to a lapse in detection of static signatures, only for this malicious content to be revealed (i.e., [Deobfuscate/Decode Files or Information](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1140)) at the time of execution/use.

This type of file obfuscation can be applied to many file artifacts present on victim hosts, such as malware log/configuration and payload files.(Citation: File obfuscation) Files can be encrypted with a hardcoded or user-supplied key, as well as otherwise obfuscated using standard encoding schemes such as Base64.

The entire content of a file may be obfuscated, or just specific functions or values (such as C2 addresses). Encryption and encoding may also be applied in redundant layers for additional protection.

For example, adversaries may abuse password-protected Word documents or self-extracting (SFX) archives as a method of encrypting/encoding a file such as a [Phishing](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1566) payload. These files typically function by attaching the intended archived content to a decompressor stub that is executed when the file is invoked (e.g., [User Execution](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1204)).(Citation: SFX - Encrypted/Encoded File) 

Adversaries may also abuse file-specific as well as custom encoding schemes. For example, Byte Order Mark (BOM) headers in text files may be abused to manipulate and obfuscate file content until [Command and Scripting Interpreter](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1059) execution.
T1027.013.mitigations:
Mitigation 'Antivirus/Antimalware': Anti-virus can be used to automatically detect and quarantine suspicious files, including those with high entropy measurements or with otherwise potentially malicious signs of obfuscation.
Mitigation 'Behavior Prevention on Endpoint': On Windows 10+, enable Attack Surface Reduction (ASR) rules to block execution of potentially obfuscated scripts. Security tools should be configured to analyze the encoding properties of files and detect anomalies that deviate from standard encoding practices.
T1027.013.detections:
Detection Strategy: Encrypted or Encoded File Payload Detection Strategy
- Analytic 'Analytic 0237': Detection of processes that load or decode encrypted/encoded files in memory and subsequently execute or inject them, indicating payload unpacking or memory-resident malware.
- Analytic 'Analytic 0238': Detection of suspicious use of shell utilities or scripts that decode or decrypt a payload and execute it without writing to disk.
- Analytic 'Analytic 0239': Detection of encoded payloads being decoded and executed in-memory using scripting tools or third-party decoders.
T1027.013.procedures: Software 'Donut': Donut can generate encrypted, compressed/encoded, or otherwise obfuscated code modules. Software 'PcShare': PcShare has been encrypted with XOR using different 32-long Base16 strings. Software 'Sliver': Sliver can encrypt strings at compile time. Software 'Remcos': Remcos can use string encryption to hinder analysis. Software 'DCRAT': The DCRAT configuration file is encrypted using AES-256. Software 'IronNetInjector': IronNetInjector can obfuscate variable names, encrypt strings, as well as base64 encode and Rijndael encrypt payloads. Software 'DEADEYE': DEADEYE has encrypted its payload. Software 'Qilin': Qilin can employ several code obfuscation methods, including renaming functions, altering control flows, and encrypting strings. Software 'Torisma': Torisma has been Base64 encoded and AES encrypted. Software 'OSX_OCEANLOTUS.D': OSX_OCEANLOTUS.D encrypts its strings in RSA256 and encodes them in a custom base64 scheme and XOR. Software 'USBStealer': Most strings in USBStealer are encrypted using 3DES and XOR and reversed. Software 'Emissary': Variants of Emissary encrypt payloads using various XOR ciphers, as well as a custom algorithm that uses the "srand" and "rand" functions. Software 'Cuckoo Stealer': Cuckoo Stealer strings are XOR-encrypted. Software 'Kessel': Kessel's configuration is hardcoded and RC4 encrypted within the binary. Software 'Raindrop': Raindrop encrypted its payload using a simple XOR algorithm with a single-byte key. Software 'Rifdoor': Rifdoor has encrypted strings with a single byte XOR algorithm. Software 'Shark': Shark can use encrypted and encoded files for C2 configuration. Software 'Ursnif': Ursnif has used an XOR-based algorithm to encrypt Tor clients dropped to disk. Ursnif droppers have also been delivered as password-protected zip files that execute base64 encoded PowerShell commands. Software 'RustyWater': RustyWater has encrypted all strings in the code using position independent XOR encryption. Software 'ROADSWEEP': The ROADSWEEP binary contains RC4 encrypted embedded scripts. Software 'PAKLOG': PAKLOG has utilized a simple encoding mechanism to encode characters in the buffer. Software 'RansomHub': RansomHub has an encrypted configuration file. Software 'Ninja': The Ninja payload is XOR encrypted and compressed. Ninja has also XORed its configuration data with a constant value of `0xAA`. Software 'HAWKBALL': HAWKBALL has encrypted the payload with an XOR-based algorithm. Software 'Skidmap': Skidmap has encrypted it's main payload using 3DES. Software 'Astaroth': Astaroth has used an XOR-based algorithm to encrypt payloads twice with different keys. Software 'KEYPLUG': KEYPLUG can use a hardcoded one-byte XOR encoded configuration file. Software 'Sakula': Sakula uses single-byte XOR obfuscation to obfuscate many of its files. Software 'Zox': Zox has been encoded with Base64. Software 'Helminth': The Helminth config file is encrypted with RC4. Software 'SamSam': SamSam has been seen using AES or DES to encrypt payloads and payload components. Software 'Prikormka': Some resources in Prikormka are encrypted with a simple XOR operation or encoded with Base64. Software 'NanHaiShu': NanHaiShu encodes files in Base64. Software 'metaMain': metaMain's module file has been encrypted via XOR. Software 'Medusa Ransomware': Medusa Ransomware has utilized XOR encrypted strings. Software 'Bazar': Bazar has used XOR, RSA2, and RC4 encrypted files. Software 'LockBit 3.0': The LockBit 3.0 payload includes an encrypted main component. Software 'LoudMiner': LoudMiner has encrypted DMG files. Software 'DOWNIISSA': DOWNIISSA code is base64 encoded and XOR encrypted. Software 'MEDUSA': MEDUSA can XOR encrypt configuration strings. Software 'BLUELIGHT': BLUELIGHT has a XOR-encoded payload. Software 'PipeMon': PipeMon modules are stored encrypted on disk. Software 'Woody RAT': Woody RAT has used Base64 encoded strings and scripts. Software 'DCSrv': DCSrv's configuration is encrypted. Software 'HiddenFace': HiddenFace has encrypted its payload with AES. Software 'GravityRAT': GravityRAT supports file encryption (AES with the key "lolomycin2017"). Software 'Metamorfo': Metamorfo has encrypted payloads and strings. Software 'XLoader': XLoader features encrypted functions using the RC4 algorithm and bytecode operations. Software 'Sagerunex': Sagerunex can be passed a reference to an XOR-encrypted configuration file at runtime. Software 'StrelaStealer': StrelaStealer uses XOR-encoded strings to obfuscate items. Software 'LightNeuron': LightNeuron encrypts its configuration files with AES-256. Software 'DanBot': DanBot can Base64 encode its payload. Software 'FELIXROOT': FELIXROOT encrypts strings in the backdoor using a custom XOR algorithm. Software 'Remsec': Some data in Remsec is encrypted using RC5 in CBC mode, AES-CBC with a hardcoded key, RC4, or Salsa20. Some data is also base64-encoded. Software 'SocGholish': SocGholish has single or double Base-64 encoded references to its second-stage server URLs. Software 'NOOPLDR': The NOOPLDR payload is encrypted with AES256-CBC. Software 'DUSTTRAP': DUSTTRAP begins with an initial launcher that decrypts an AES-128-CFB encrypted file on disk and executes it in memory. Software 'MOPSLED': MOPSLED can encrypt configuration files with a custom ChaCha20 algorithm. Software 'WindTail': WindTail can be delivered as a compressed, encrypted, and encoded payload. Software 'Grandoreiro': The Grandoreiro payload has been delivered encrypted with a custom XOR-based algorithm and also as a base64-encoded ZIP file. Software 'Milan': Milan can encode files containing information about the targeted system. Software 'BLINDINGCAN': BLINDINGCAN has obfuscated code using Base64 encoding. Software 'KONNI': KONNI is heavily obfuscated and includes encrypted configuration files. Software 'TINYTYPHON': TINYTYPHON has used XOR with 0x90 to obfuscate its configuration file. Software 'WhisperGate': WhisperGate can Base64 encode strings, store downloaded files in reverse byte order, and use the Eazfuscator tool to obfuscate its third stage. Software 'ZxxZ': ZxxZ has been encoded to avoid detection from static analysis tools. Software 'DarkGate': DarkGate drops an encrypted PE file, pe.bin, and decrypts it during installation. DarkGate also uses custom base64 encoding schemas in later variations to obfuscate payloads. Software 'Reaver': Reaver encrypts some of its files with XOR. Software 'BOOSTWRITE': BOOSTWRITE has encoded its payloads using a ChaCha stream cipher with a 256-bit key and 64-bit Initialization vector (IV) to evade detection. Software 'Fysbis': Fysbis has been encrypted using XOR and RC4. Software 'Stuxnet': Stuxnet uses encrypted configuration blocks and writes encrypted files to disk. Software 'BitPaymer': BitPaymer has used RC4-encrypted strings and string hashes to avoid identifiable strings within the binary. Software 'Kwampirs': Kwampirs downloads additional files that are base64-encoded and encrypted with another cipher. Software 'Remexi': Remexi obfuscates its configuration data with XOR. Software 'TYPEFRAME': APIs and strings in some TYPEFRAME variants are RC4 encrypted. Another variant is encoded with XOR. Software 'Mango': Mango contains a series of base64 encoded substrings. Software 'FoggyWeb': FoggyWeb has been XOR-encoded. Software 'StrongPity': StrongPity has used encrypted strings in its dropper component. Software 'GlassWorm': GlassWorm has leveraged AES-256-CBC encryption to obfuscate its malicious JavaScript payload. GlassWorm has also utilized Base64 encoding to obfuscate the C2 details stored in the Solana memo field. Software 'LightSpy': LightSpy encrypts the C2 configuration file using AES with a static key, while the module `.dylib` files use a rolling one-byte encoding for obfuscation. Software 'MagicRAT': MagicRAT stores base64 encoded command and contorl URLs in a configuraiton file, with each URL prefixed with the value `LR02DPt22R`. Software 'BRICKSTORM': BRICKSTORM has utilized XOR cipher encryption to hide key strings within their code, to include IPv4 addresses of public DNS-over-HTTPS (DOH) servers. Software 'VersaMem': VersaMem encrypted captured credentials with AES then Base64 encoded them before writing to local storage. Software 'Bisonal': Bisonal's DLL file and non-malicious decoy file are encrypted with RC4 and some function name strings are obfuscated. Software 'Raccoon Stealer': Raccoon Stealer uses RC4 encryption for strings and command and control addresses to evade static detection. Software 'LODEINFO': The LODEINFO loader module contains XOR-encrypted shellcode. Software 'RedLeaves': A RedLeaves configuration file is encrypted with a simple XOR key, 0x53. Software 'PHASEJAM': PHASEJAM has launched a webshell using the `MIME::Base64` module that encoded and decoded Base64 commands. Software 'AshTag': The AshTag AshenOrchestrator component payload as been Base64 encoded and embedded with HTML content from the C2 server. Software 'UPSTYLE': UPSTYLE stores primary content as base64-encoded objects. Software 'FlawedGrace': FlawedGrace encrypts its C2 configuration files with AES in CBC mode. Software 'More_eggs': More_eggs's payload has been encrypted with a key that has the hostname and processor family information appended to the end. Software 'Elise': Elise encrypts several of its files, including configuration files. Software 'Avenger': Avenger has the ability to XOR encrypt files to be sent to C2. Software 'EnvyScout': EnvyScout can Base64 encode payloads. Software 'SplatDropper': SplatDropper has also utilized XOR encrypted payload. Software 'Rising Sun': Configuration data used by Rising Sun has been encrypted using an RC4 stream algorithm. Software 'HeartCrypt': HeartCrypt strings are encrypted via a single-byte XOR operation rotating over a hard-coded key, possibly provided by the PaaS customers. Software 'LunarMail': LunarMail has used RC4 and AES to encrypt strings and its exfiltration configuration respectively. Software 'ANELLDR': ANELLDR can update its encryption key to AES-256-CBC and re-encrypt its payload, overwriting the original payload file with the newly encrypted data. Software 'Mispadu': Mispadu uses a custom algorithm to obfuscate its internal strings and uses hardcoded keys. Mispadu also uses encoded configuration files and has encoded payloads using Base64. Software 'Dacls': Dacls can encrypt its configuration file with AES CBC. Software 'njRAT': njRAT has included a base64 encoded executable. Software 'Kerrdown': Kerrdown can encrypt, encode, and compress multiple layers of shellcode. Software 'BeaverTail': BeaverTail has obfuscated strings of code with Base64 encoding within the JavaScript version of the malware. BeaverTail has also utilized the open-source tool JavaScript-Obfuscator to obfuscate strings and functions. Software 'CorKLOG': CorKLOG has encrypted collected contents using RC4. CorKLOG has also utilized XOR encrypted strings. Software 'IPsec Helper': IPsec Helper contains an embedded XML configuration file with an encrypted list of command and control servers. These are written to an external configuration file during execution. Software 'StealBit': StealBit stores obfuscated DLL file names in its executable. Software 'DUSTPAN': DUSTPAN decrypts an embedded payload. Software 'HexEval Loader': HexEval Loader has encoded module names and C2 URLs as hexadecimal strings in attempts to evade analysis. Software 'PS1': PS1 is distributed as a set of encrypted files and scripts. Software 'KeyBoy': In one version of KeyBoy, string obfuscation routines were used to hide many of the critical values referenced in the malware. Software 'RainyDay': RainyDay has downloaded as a XOR-encrypted payload. Software 'Waterbear': Waterbear has used RC4 encrypted shellcode and encrypted functions. Software 'RedLine Stealer': RedLine Stealer has encrypted and encoded configuration data with Base64 and XOR functions. Software 'Winnti for Linux': Winnti for Linux can encode its configuration file with single-byte XOR encoding. Software 'Seasalt': Seasalt obfuscates configuration data. Software 'StoneDrill': StoneDrill has obfuscated its module with an alphabet-based table or XOR encryption. Software 'IcedID': IcedID has utilzed encrypted binaries and base64 encoded strings. Software 'Tsundere Botnet': Tsundere Botnet’s loader contained AES-CBC/PKCS7 encrypted blobs, which were descrypted and written to disk. Software 'DEADWOOD': DEADWOOD contains an embedded, AES-encrypted resource named METADATA that contains configuration information for follow-on execution. Software 'SysUpdate': SysUpdate can encrypt and encode its configuration file. Software 'Chrommme': Chrommme can encrypt sections of its code to evade detection. Software 'LunarWeb': The LunarWeb install files have been encrypted with AES-256. Software 'Attor': Strings in Attor's components are encrypted with a XOR cipher, using a hardcoded key and the configuration data, log files and plugins are encrypted using a hybrid encryption scheme of Blowfish-OFB combined with RSA. Software 'Caminho': Caminho can use code flattening for payload obfuscation. Software 'REvil': REvil has used encrypted strings and configuration files. Software 'HOMEFRY': Some strings in HOMEFRY are obfuscated with XOR x56. Software 'SPAWNCHIMERA': SPAWNCHIMERA has encoded a private key with XOR. SPAWNCHIMERA has also encrypted data to be extracted using AES encryption. Software 'Embargo': Embargo has encrypted both MDeployer and MS4 Killer payloads with RC4. Software 'Smoke Loader': Smoke Loader uses a simple one-byte XOR method to obfuscate values in the malware. Software 'Aria-body': Aria-body has used an encrypted configuration file for its loader. Software 'Mosquito': Mosquito’s installer is obfuscated with a custom crypter to obfuscate the installer. Software 'CozyCar': The payload of CozyCar is encrypted with simple XOR with a rotating key. The CozyCar configuration file has been encrypted with RC4 keys. Software 'YAHOYAH': YAHOYAH encrypts its configuration file using a simple algorithm. Software 'ThreatNeedle': ThreatNeedle has been compressed and obfuscated using RC4, AES, or XOR. Software 'BlackByte Ransomware': BlackByte Ransomware is distributed as an encrypted payload. Software 'BendyBear': BendyBear has encrypted payloads using RC4 and XOR. Software 'PyDCrypt': PyDCrypt has been compiled and encrypted with PyInstaller, specifically using the --key flag during the build phase. Software 'VERMIN': VERMIN is obfuscated using the obfuscation tool called ConfuserEx. Software 'Micropsia': Micropsia obfuscates the configuration with a custom Base64 and XOR. Software 'Kevin': Kevin has Base64-encoded its configuration file. Software 'ROAMINGHOUSE': ROAMINGHOUSE can embed a ZIP file containing UPPERCUT components into three base64 encoded parts. Software 'JHUHUGIT': Many strings in JHUHUGIT are obfuscated with a XOR algorithm. Software 'GoldMax': GoldMax has written AES-encrypted and Base64-encoded configuration files to disk. Software 'Chinoxy': Chinoxy has encrypted its configuration file. Software 'DOGCALL': DOGCALL is encrypted using single-byte XOR. Software 'Heyoka Backdoor': Heyoka Backdoor can encrypt its payload. Software 'BOOKWORM': BOOKWORM has utilized Base64 encoding to obfuscate its payload. Software 'FIVEHANDS': The FIVEHANDS payload is encrypted with AES-128. Software 'DRYHOOK': DRYHOOK has encrypted stolen credentials strings within a file using both Base64 and RC4 with a hard-coded key. Software 'Zeus Panda': Zeus Panda encrypts strings with XOR. Zeus Panda also encrypts all configuration and settings in AES and RC4. Software 'Squirrelwaffle': Squirrelwaffle has been obfuscated with a XOR-based algorithm. Software 'HyperBro': HyperBro can be delivered encrypted to a compromised host. Software 'STARWHALE': STARWHALE has been obfuscated with hex-encoded strings. Software 'HotCroissant': HotCroissant has encrypted strings with single-byte XOR and base64 encoded RC4. Software 'Lumma Stealer': Lumma Stealer has used AES-encrypted payloads contained within PowerShell scripts. Software 'KGH_SPY': KGH_SPY has used encrypted strings in its installer. Software 'SUPERNOVA': SUPERNOVA contained Base64-encoded strings. Software 'Carberp': Carberp has used XOR-based encryption to mask C2 server locations within the trojan. Software 'Emotet': Emotet uses obfuscated URLs to download a ZIP file. Software 'SpeakUp': SpeakUp encodes its second-stage payload with Base64. Software 'XORIndex Loader': XORIndex Loader has encoded module names and C2 URLs as hexadecimal strings in attempts to evade analysis. Software 'PHPsert': PHPsert can use multiple obfuscation techniques including XOR encoding, hexadecimal character representation, string concatenation, and randomized variable names. Software 'Mafalda': Mafalda has been obfuscated and contains encrypted functions. Software 'Hi-Zor': Hi-Zor uses various XOR techniques to obfuscate its components. Software 'HiddenWasp': HiddenWasp encrypts its configuration and payload. Software 'Latrodectus': Latrodectus has used a pseudo random number generator (PRNG) algorithm and a rolling XOR key to obfuscate strings. Software 'GoldenSpy': GoldenSpy's uninstaller has base64-encoded its variables. Software 'Taidoor': Taidoor can use encrypted string blocks for obfuscation. Software 'WastedLocker': The WastedLocker payload includes encrypted strings stored within the .bss section of the binary file. Software 'Winnti for Windows': Winnti for Windows has the ability to encrypt and compress its payload. Software 'XCSSET': Older XCSSET variants use `xxd` to encode modules. Later versions pass an `xxd` or `base64` encoded blob through multiple decoding stages to reconstruct the module name, AppleScript, or shell command. For example, the initial network request uses three layers of hex decoding before executing a curl command in a shell. Software 'Exaramel for Linux': Exaramel for Linux uses RC4 for encrypting the configuration. Software 'HermeticWizard': HermeticWizard has the ability to encrypt PE files with a reverse XOR loop. Software 'Gazer': Gazer logs its actions into files that are encrypted with 3DES. It also uses RSA to encrypt resources. Software 'Hildegard': Hildegard has encrypted an ELF file. Software 'Volgmer': A Volgmer variant is encoded using a simple XOR cipher. Software 'Kapeka': Kapeka utilizes AES-256 (CBC mode), XOR, and RSA-2048 encryption schemas for various configuration and other objects. Software 'InvisibleFerret': InvisibleFerret has utilized the XOR and Base64 encoding for each of its modules. InvisibleFerret has also obfuscated files with a combination of zlib, Base64 and reverse string order. InvisibleFerret has also utilized the XOR and Base64 encoding some of its Python scripts. Software 'TrickBot': TrickBot uses an AES CBC (256 bits) encryption algorithm for its loader and configuration files. Software 'RAPIDPULSE': RAPIDPULSE has the ability to RC4 encrypt and base64 encode decrypted files on compromised servers prior to writing them to stdout. Software 'LP-Notes': LP-Notes has used a custom addition-based function and a string stacking function for string encryption. Software 'Uroburos': Uroburos can use AES and CAST-128 encryption to obfuscate resources. Software 'FunnyDream': FunnyDream can Base64 encode its C2 address stored in a template binary with the `xyz0123456789ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZabcdefghijklmnopqrstuvw_-` or `xyz0123456789ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZabcdefghijklmnopqrstuvw_=` character sets. Software 'PlugX': PlugX has leveraged XOR encryption with the key of 123456789. Software 'AuditCred': AuditCred encrypts the configuration. Software 'Penquin': Penquin has encrypted strings in the binary for obfuscation. Software 'PureCrypter': PureCrypter has used SmartAssembly and NET-Reactor for string encryption and control flow obfuscation. Software 'GreyEnergy': GreyEnergy encrypts its configuration files with AES-256 and also encrypts its strings. Software 'CARROTBAT': CARROTBAT has the ability to download a base64 encoded payload. Software 'ZeroT': ZeroT has encrypted its payload with RC4. Software 'Cardinal RAT': Cardinal RAT encodes many of its artifacts and is encrypted (AES-128) when downloaded. Group 'Inception': Inception has encrypted malware payloads dropped on victim machines with AES and RC4 encryption. Group 'Dark Caracal': Dark Caracal has obfuscated strings in Bandook by base64 encoding, and then encrypting them. Group 'Elderwood': Elderwood has encrypted documents and malicious executables. Group 'Darkhotel': Darkhotel has obfuscated code using RC4, XOR, and RSA. Group 'Transparent Tribe': Transparent Tribe has dropped encoded executables on compromised hosts. Group 'APT28': APT28 encrypted a .dll payload using RTL and a custom encryption algorithm. APT28 has also obfuscated payloads with base64, XOR, and RC4. Group 'APT18': APT18 obfuscates strings in the payload. Group 'Leviathan': Leviathan has obfuscated code using base64. Group 'Sidewinder': Sidewinder has used base64 encoding and ECDH-P256 encryption for payloads. Group 'APT39': APT39 has used malware to drop encrypted CAB files. Group 'Lazarus Group': Lazarus Group has used multiple types of encryption and encoding for their payloads, including AES, Caracachs, RC4, XOR, Base64, and other tricks such as creating aliases in code for Native API function names.During Operation Dream Job, Lazarus Group encrypted malware such as DRATzarus with XOR and DLL files with base64. Group 'Saint Bear': Saint Bear initial payloads included encoded follow-on payloads located in the resources file of the first-stage loader. Group 'APT33': APT33 has used base64 to encode payloads. Group 'Kimsuky': Kimsuky has obfuscated code within files by converting hexadecimal strings to decimal numbers using the `CLng function` in combination with processing arithmetic operations and leveraging the `Chr function` to generate readable characters. Kimsuky has also encoded files with Base64 and RC4. Kimsuky has utilized XOR and RC4 to encode malicious payloads. Group 'BITTER': BITTER has used a RAR SFX dropper to deliver malware. Group 'TA505': TA505 has password-protected malicious Word documents. Group 'Higaisa': Higaisa used Base64 encoded compressed payloads. Group 'APT19': APT19 used Base64 to obfuscate payloads. Group 'APT-C-36': APT-C-36 has used encoded and obfuscated files, images, and executables. Group 'Fox Kitten': Fox Kitten has base64 encoded payloads to avoid detection. Group 'Threat Group-3390': A Threat Group-3390 tool can encrypt payloads using XOR. Threat Group-3390 malware is also obfuscated using Metasploit’s shikata_ga_nai encoder. Group 'TA2541': TA2541 has used compressed and char-encoded scripts in operations. Group 'Malteiro': Malteiro has used scripts encoded in Base64 certificates to distribute malware to victims. Group 'Magic Hound': Magic Hound malware has used base64-encoded files and has also encrypted embedded strings with AES. Group 'Storm-1811': Storm-1811 XOR encodes a Cobalt Strike installation payload in a DLL file that is decoded with a hardcoded key when called by a legitimate 7zip installation process. Group 'Blue Mockingbird': Blue Mockingbird has obfuscated the wallet address in the payload binary. Group 'Tropic Trooper': Tropic Trooper has encrypted configuration files. Group 'Mofang': Mofang has encrypted payloads before they are downloaded to victims. Group 'Contagious Interview': Contagious Interview has used hexadecimal string encoding to hide critical JavaScript module names, function names, and C2 URLs, which are decoded dynamically at runtime. Group 'Whitefly': Whitefly has encrypted the payload used for C2. Group 'menuPass': menuPass has encoded strings in its malware with base64 as well as with a simple, single-byte XOR obfuscation using key 0x40. Group 'Moses Staff': Moses Staff has used obfuscated web shells in their operations. Group 'TeamTNT': TeamTNT has encrypted its binaries via AES and encoded files using Base64. Group 'Metador': Metador has encrypted their payloads. Group 'Group5': Group5 disguised its malicious binaries with several layers of obfuscation, including encrypting the files. Group 'Putter Panda': Droppers used by Putter Panda use RC4 or a 16-byte XOR key consisting of the bytes 0xA0 – 0xAF to obfuscate payloads. Group 'OilRig': OilRig has encrypted and encoded data in its malware, including by using base64.During Outer Space, OilRig deployed VBS droppers with obfuscated strings. Group 'APT32': APT32 has performed code obfuscation, including encoding payloads using Base64 and using a framework called "Dont-Kill-My-Cat (DKMC). APT32 also encrypts the library used for network exfiltration with AES-256 in CBC mode in their macOS backdoor. Group 'MirrorFace': MirrorFace has used Base64 encoded shellcode in infection chains to evade detection. Group 'Moonstone Sleet': Moonstone Sleet has used encrypted payloads within files for follow-on execution and defense evasion. Group 'APT41': APT41 DUST was conducted by APT41 from 2023 to July 2024.APT41 DUST used encrypted payloads decrypted and executed in memory. Group 'Mustang Panda': RedDelta Modified PlugX Infection Chain Operations was an campaign linked to Mustang Panda operations.Mustang Panda stored installation payloads as encrypted files in hidden folders during RedDelta Modified PlugX Infection Chain Operations. Group 'AppleJeus': During the 3CX Supply Chain Attack, AppleJeus encrypts its dynamic library files (.dll) using RC4, and when loaded only decrypts specific portions of the file using the key `3jB(2bsG#@c7`. Group 'UNC3886': In mid-2024 Mandiant identified custom TINYSHELL-based backdoors deployed on Juniper Networks’ Junos OS routers. Mandiant attributed these backdoors to the China-nexus espionage group UNC3886.During RedPenguin, UNC3886 generated Base64-encoded files in the FreeBSD shell environment of targeted Juniper devices. Campaign 'ShadowRay': During ShadowRay, threat actors used Base64-encrypted Python code to evade detection. Campaign 'Night Dragon': During Night Dragon, threat actors used a DLL that included an XOR-encoded section. Campaign 'Operation Honeybee': During Operation Honeybee, the threat actors used Base64 to encode files with a custom key. Campaign '2025 Poland Wiper Attacks': During the 2025 Poland Wiper Attacks, the adversaries utilized a Base64-encoded ZIP archive to prevent content analysis. Campaign 'APT41 DUST': APT41 DUST used encrypted payloads decrypted and executed in memory. Campaign 'RedDelta Modified PlugX Infection Chain Operations': Mustang Panda stored installation payloads as encrypted files in hidden folders during RedDelta Modified PlugX Infection Chain Operations. Campaign 'Operation Dust Storm': During Operation Dust Storm, the threat actors encoded some payloads with a single-byte XOR, both skipping the key itself and zeroing in an attempt to avoid exposing the key; other payloads were Base64-encoded. Campaign 'Outer Space': During Outer Space, OilRig deployed VBS droppers with obfuscated strings. Campaign 'Operation Dream Job': During Operation Dream Job, Lazarus Group encrypted malware such as DRATzarus with XOR and DLL files with base64. Campaign '3CX Supply Chain Attack': During the 3CX Supply Chain Attack, AppleJeus encrypts its dynamic library files (.dll) using RC4, and when loaded only decrypts specific portions of the file using the key `3jB(2bsG#@c7`. Campaign 'RedPenguin': During RedPenguin, UNC3886 generated Base64-encoded files in the FreeBSD shell environment of targeted Juniper devices. Campaign 'Operation Spalax': For Operation Spalax, the threat actors used XOR-encrypted payloads. Campaign 'Cutting Edge': During Cutting Edge, threat actors used a Base64-encoded Python script to write a patched version of the Ivanti Connect Secure `dsls` binary.

CVE-2026-33017.title: Langflow Code Injection Vulnerability
CVE-2026-33017.description: Langflow is a tool for building and deploying AI-powered agents and workflows. In versions prior to 1.9.0, the POST /api/v1/build_public_tmp/{flow_id}/flow endpoint allows building public flows without requiring authentication. When the optional data parameter is supplied, the endpoint uses attacker-controlled flow data (containing arbitrary Python code in node definitions) instead of the stored flow data from the database. This code is passed to exec() with zero sandboxing, resulting in unauthenticated remote code execution. This is distinct from CVE-2025-3248, which fixed /api/v1/validate/code by adding authentication. The build_public_tmp endpoint is designed to be unauthenticated (for public flows) but incorrectly accepts attacker-supplied flow data containing arbitrary executable code. This issue has been fixed in version 1.9.0.
CVE-2026-33017.mitigations: Apply mitigations per vendor instructions, follow applicable BOD 22-01 guidance for cloud services, or discontinue use of the product if mitigations are unavailable. Consultar las referencias: https://github.com/langflow-ai/langflow/releases/tag/1.8.2

CVE-2025-3248.title: Langflow Missing Authentication Vulnerability
CVE-2025-3248.description:
Langflow versions prior to 1.3.0 are susceptible to code injection in 
the /api/v1/validate/code endpoint. A remote and unauthenticated attacker can send crafted HTTP requests to execute arbitrary
code.
CVE-2025-3248.mitigations: Apply mitigations per vendor instructions, follow applicable BOD 22-01 guidance for cloud services, or discontinue use of the product if mitigations are unavailable. Consultar las referencias: https://github.com/langflow-ai/langflow/pull/6911

CVE-2022-24112.title: Apache APISIX Authentication Bypass Vulnerability
CVE-2022-24112.description: An attacker can abuse the batch-requests plugin to send requests to bypass the IP restriction of Admin API. A default configuration of Apache APISIX (with default API key) is vulnerable to remote code execution. When the admin key was changed or the port of Admin API was changed to a port different from the data panel, the impact is lower. But there is still a risk to bypass the IP restriction of Apache APISIX's data panel. There is a check in the batch-requests plugin which overrides the client IP with its real remote IP. But due to a bug in the code, this check can be bypassed.

mitre_evaluations.explanation: The alert contains a Base64 encoded payload within a JNDI string, which requires decoding to execute the shell script. This technique is directly relevant to the deobfuscation process.
While the attack involves a multi-stage process (JNDI lookup to shell script), this technique is less specific than others identified.
The attack is an automated exploit against an API endpoint, not a user-driven copy-paste action.
The payload in the log is explicitly Base64 encoded, which is a standard encoding scheme used to obfuscate the malicious command.
The attack is delivered via an HTTP POST request to an API endpoint, which is a classic use of web protocols for C2/exploitation.
There is no evidence of traffic signaling or port knocking in the provided log.
While the attack uses an external resource, it is a direct JNDI/LDAP callback rather than a dead drop resolver on a legitimate web service.
The payload explicitly uses 'curl' to download and execute a shell script from a remote server, which is a clear example of Ingress Tool Transfer.
No symmetric cryptography is observed in the provided log.
The payload is encoded/obfuscated to bypass detection, which aligns with the intent of this technique.

cve_evaluations.explanation: While this is a recent code injection vulnerability, the alert log shows a JNDI/Log4Shell-style injection, not a Langflow-specific API exploit.
Similar to the other Langflow CVE, this does not match the JNDI/LDAP pattern observed in the log.
This CVE is unrelated to the JNDI/LDAP injection pattern observed in the alert.

rule.description: Critical security event: Anomalous behavior or exploit payload detected by system monitoring.

mitre.description: An external source is attempting to send a malicious payload targeting the SMBv1 protocol on a core infrastructure node, specifically triggering an exploit signature related to FEA List Overflow.
mitre.keywords: exploit payload, SMBv1 FEA List Overflow, remote code execution attempt

cve.description: The alert identifies an exploitation attempt targeting the SMBv1 protocol and the srv.sys driver, which is associated with the MS17-010 vulnerability.

T1190.name: Exploit Public-Facing Application
T1190.description:
Adversaries may attempt to exploit a weakness in an Internet-facing host or system to initially access a network. The weakness in the system can be a software bug, a temporary glitch, or a misconfiguration.

Exploited applications are often websites/web servers, but can also include databases (like SQL), standard services (like SMB or SSH), network device administration and management protocols (like SNMP and Smart Install), and any other system with Internet-accessible open sockets.(Citation: NVD CVE-2016-6662)(Citation: CIS Multiple SMB Vulnerabilities)(Citation: US-CERT TA18-106A Network Infrastructure Devices 2018)(Citation: Cisco Blog Legacy Device Attacks)(Citation: NVD CVE-2014-7169) On ESXi infrastructure, adversaries may exploit exposed OpenSLP services; they may alternatively exploit exposed VMware vCenter servers.(Citation: Recorded Future ESXiArgs Ransomware 2023)(Citation: Ars Technica VMWare Code Execution Vulnerability 2021) Depending on the flaw being exploited, this may also involve [Exploitation for Stealth](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1211) or [Exploitation for Client Execution](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1203).

If an application is hosted on cloud-based infrastructure and/or is containerized, then exploiting it may lead to compromise of the underlying instance or container. This can allow an adversary a path to access the cloud or container APIs (e.g., via the [Cloud Instance Metadata API](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1552/005)), exploit container host access via [Escape to Host](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1611), or take advantage of weak identity and access management policies.

Adversaries may also exploit edge network infrastructure and related appliances, specifically targeting devices that do not support robust host-based defenses.(Citation: Mandiant Fortinet Zero Day)(Citation: Wired Russia Cyberwar)

For websites and databases, the OWASP top 10 and CWE top 25 highlight the most common web-based vulnerabilities.(Citation: OWASP Top 10)(Citation: CWE top 25)
T1190.mitigations:
Mitigation 'Application Isolation and Sandboxing': Application isolation will limit what other processes and system features the exploited target can access.
Mitigation 'Filter Network Traffic': Restrict outbound network traffic from public-facing servers to prevent unauthorized connections from initiating communications with attacker-controlled infrastructure. While this may not prevent the initial exploitation, it limits the attacker's ability to verify and control the compromised server post-exploit, reducing the overall impact of the attack.
Mitigation 'Network Segmentation': Segment externally facing servers and services from the rest of the network with a DMZ or on separate hosting infrastructure.
Mitigation 'Vulnerability Scanning': Regularly scan externally facing systems for vulnerabilities and establish procedures to rapidly patch systems when critical vulnerabilities are discovered through scanning and through public disclosure.
Mitigation 'Privileged Account Management': Use least privilege for service accounts will limit what permissions the exploited process gets on the rest of the system.
Mitigation 'Exploit Protection': Web Application Firewalls may be used to limit exposure of applications to prevent exploit traffic from reaching the application.
Mitigation 'Limit Access to Resource Over Network': Ensure that all publicly exposed services are actually intended to be so, and restrict access to any that should only be available internally.
Mitigation 'Update Software': Update software regularly by employing patch management for externally exposed applications.
T1190.detections:
Detection Strategy: Exploit Public-Facing Application – multi-signal correlation (request → error → post-exploit process/egress)
- Analytic 'Analytic 0219': Adversary sends crafted HTTP/S (or other service) input to an Internet-facing app (IIS/ASP.NET, API, device portal). Chain: (1) abnormal request patterns to public endpoint → (2) elevated 4xx/5xx or unusual methods/paths → (3) server process (w3wp.exe/other service) spawns shell/LOLbins or loads non-standard modules → (4) optional outbound callback from the host/container.
- Analytic 'Analytic 0220': Adversary exploits Apache/Nginx/app servers. Chain: (1) suspicious requests in access logs → (2) spike of 5xx or WAF blocks → (3) web server or interpreter (apache2/nginx/php-fpm/node/python) spawns /bin/sh, curl, wget, socat, or writes webshell → (4) outbound callback.
- Analytic 'Analytic 0221': Adversary targets macOS-hosted public services (e.g., nginx, node). Chain: suspicious inbound request → service crash/5xx → service spawns shell or writes file → new outbound connection.
- Analytic 'Analytic 0222': Adversary exploits containerized app via ingress or service. Chain: (1) suspicious request in ingress/app logs → (2) container process spawns a shell/exec/sidecar (kubectl exec/docker exec) → (3) egress to Internet or metadata service (169.254.169.254).
- Analytic 'Analytic 0223': Adversary targets cloud-hosted public endpoints. Chain: (1) ALB/ELB/Cloud LB logs show exploit-like inputs or error spikes → (2) workload spawns shell or reaches metadata API → (3) egress to new external hosts.
- Analytic 'Analytic 0224': Adversary exploits exposed OpenSLP on ESXi or vCenter public endpoints. Chain: inbound request pattern to mgmt service → hostd/vpxd error/crash/restart → unexpected process behavior or datastore access → outbound callback.
- Analytic 'Analytic 0225': Adversary exploits public admin services on routers/firewalls/switches. Chain: anomalous HTTP/SNMP/SmartInstall inputs → device syslog errors/restarts → config changes/CLI spawn → egress to attacker C2.
T1190.procedures:
Software 'sqlmap': sqlmap can be used to automate exploitation of SQL injection vulnerabilities.
Software 'Havij': Havij is used to automate SQL injection.
Software 'Siloscape': Siloscape is executed after the attacker gains initial access to a Windows container using a known vulnerability.
Software 'SoreFang': SoreFang can gain access by exploiting a Sangfor SSL VPN vulnerability that allows for the placement and delivery of malicious update binaries.
Software 'ZxShell': ZxShell has been dropped through exploitation of CVE-2011-2462, CVE-2013-3163, and CVE-2014-0322.
Software 'COATHANGER': COATHANGER is installed following exploitation of a vulnerable FortiGate device.
Software 'Qilin': Qilin has been delivered through exploitation of exposed applications and interfaces including Citrix and RDP.
Software 'BOLDMOVE': BOLDMOVE is associated with exploitation of CVE-2022-49475 in FortiOS.
Group 'Rocke': Rocke exploited Apache Struts, Oracle WebLogic (CVE-2017-10271), and Adobe ColdFusion (CVE-2017-3066) vulnerabilities to deliver malware.
Group 'Threat Group-3390': Threat Group-3390 has exploited the Microsoft SharePoint vulnerability CVE-2019-0604 and CVE-2021-26855, CVE-2021-26857, CVE-2021-26858, and CVE-2021-27065 in Exchange Server.
Group 'FIN7': FIN7 has compromised targeted organizations through exploitation of CVE-2021-31207 in Exchange.
Group 'Volt Typhoon': Volt Typhoon has gained initial access through exploitation of multiple vulnerabilities in internet-facing software and appliances such as Fortinet, Ivanti (formerly Pulse Secure), NETGEAR, Citrix, and Cisco.Versa Director Zero Day Exploitation was conducted by Volt Typhoon between June and August 2024.Versa Director Zero Day Exploitation involved exploitation of a vulnerability in Versa Director servers, since identified as CVE-2024-39717, for initial access and code execution.
Group 'VOID MANTICORE': VOID MANTICORE has exploited public facing vulnerabilities within victim environments to include SharePoint CVE-2019-0604.For HomeLand Justice, threat actors exploited CVE-2019-0604 in Microsoft SharePoint for initial access.
Group 'Sandworm Team': Sandworm Team exploits public-facing applications for initial access and to acquire infrastructure, such as exploitation of the EXIM mail transfer agent in Linux systems.
Group 'APT28': APT28 has used a variety of public exploits, including CVE 2020-0688 and CVE 2020-17144, to gain execution on vulnerable Microsoft Exchange; they have also conducted SQL injection attacks against external websites.
Group 'Kimsuky': Kimsuky has exploited various vulnerabilities for initial access, including Microsoft Exchange vulnerability CVE-2020-0688.
Group 'Ember Bear': Ember Bear gains initial access to victim environments by exploiting external-facing services. Examples include exploitation of CVE-2021-26084 in Confluence servers; CVE-2022-41040, ProxyShell, and other vulnerabilities in Microsoft Exchange; and multiple vulnerabilities in open-source platforms such as content management systems.
Group 'BackdoorDiplomacy': BackdoorDiplomacy has exploited CVE-2020-5902, an F5 BIP-IP vulnerability, to drop a Linux backdoor. BackdoorDiplomacy has also exploited mis-configured Plesk servers.
Group 'GOLD SOUTHFIELD': GOLD SOUTHFIELD has exploited Oracle WebLogic vulnerabilities for initial compromise.
Group 'FIN13': FIN13 has exploited known vulnerabilities such as CVE-2017-1000486 (Primefaces Application Expression Language Injection), CVE-2015-7450 (WebSphere Application Server SOAP Deserialization Exploit), CVE-2010-5326 (SAP NewWeaver Invoker Servlet Exploit), and EDB-ID-24963 (SAP NetWeaver ConfigServlet Remote Code Execution) to gain initial access.
Group 'BlackTech': BlackTech has exploited a buffer overflow vulnerability in Microsoft Internet Information Services (IIS) 6.0, CVE-2017-7269, in order to establish a new HTTP or command and control (C2) server.
Group 'Magic Hound': Magic Hound has exploited the Log4j utility (CVE-2021-44228), on-premises MS Exchange servers via "ProxyShell" (CVE-2021-34473, CVE-2021-34523, CVE-2021-31207), and Fortios SSL VPNs (CVE-2018-13379).
Group 'Medusa Group': Medusa Group has leveraged public facing vulnerabilities in their campaigns against victim organizations to gain initial access. Medusa Group has also utilized CVE-2024-1709 in ScreenConnect, and CVE-2023-48788 in Fortinet EMS for initial access to victim environments.
Group 'Sea Turtle': Sea Turtle gained access to victim environments by exploiting multiple known vulnerabilities over several campaigns.
Group 'Storm-0501': Storm-0501 has exploited N-day vulnerabilities associated with public facing services to gain initial access to victim environments to include Zoho ManageEngine (CVE-2022-47966), Citrix NetScaler “Citrix Bleed” (CVE-2023-4966), and Adobe ColdFusion 2016 (CVE-2023-29300 or CVE-2023-38203).
Group 'Fox Kitten': Fox Kitten has exploited known vulnerabilities in Fortinet, PulseSecure, and Palo Alto VPN appliances.
Group 'Cinnamon Tempest': Cinnamon Tempest has exploited multiple unpatched vulnerabilities for initial access including vulnerabilities in Microsoft Exchange, Manage Engine AdSelfService Plus, Confluence, and Log4j.
Group 'BlackByte': BlackByte exploited vulnerabilities such as ProxyLogon and ProxyShell for initial access to victim environments.

Group 'Agrius': Agrius exploits public-facing applications for initial access to victim environments. Examples include widespread attempts to exploit CVE-2018-13379 in FortiOS devices and SQL injection activity.
Group 'menuPass': menuPass has leveraged vulnerabilities in Pulse Secure VPNs to hijack sessions.
Group 'ToddyCat': ToddyCat has exploited the ProxyLogon vulnerability (CVE-2021-26855) to compromise Exchange Servers at multiple organizations.
Group 'Blue Mockingbird': Blue Mockingbird has gained initial access by exploiting CVE-2019-18935, a vulnerability within Telerik UI for ASP.NET AJAX.

Group 'Winter Vivern': Winter Vivern has exploited known and zero-day vulnerabilities in software usch as Roundcube Webmail servers and the "Follina" vulnerability.

Group 'APT29': APT29 has exploited CVE-2019-19781 for Citrix, CVE-2019-11510 for Pulse Secure VPNs, CVE-2018-13379 for FortiGate VPNs, and CVE-2019-9670 in Zimbra software to gain access.During the SolarWinds Compromise, APT29 exploited CVE-2020-0688 against the Microsoft Exchange Control Panel to regain access to a network.
Group 'Leviathan': Leviathan has used exploits against publicly-disclosed vulnerabilities for initial access into victim networks.Leviathan Australian Intrusions was conducted by the Leviathan threat actor.Leviathan exploited public-facing web applications and appliances for initial access during Leviathan Australian Intrusions.
