T1573.001.name: Symmetric Cryptography
T1573.001.description: Adversaries may employ a known symmetric encryption algorithm to conceal command and control traffic rather than relying on any inherent protections provided by a communication protocol. Symmetric encryption algorithms use the same key for plaintext encryption and ciphertext decryption. Common symmetric encryption algorithms include AES, DES, 3DES, Blowfish, and RC4.
T1573.001.mitigations: Mitigation 'Network Intrusion Prevention': Network intrusion detection and prevention systems that use network signatures to identify traffic for specific adversary malware can be used to mitigate activity at the network level.
T1573.001.detections:
Detection Strategy: Detection Strategy for Encrypted Channel via Symmetric Cryptography across OS Platforms
- Analytic 'Analytic 0400': Processes that typically do not perform cryptographic operations loading symmetric encryption libraries (e.g., bcryptprimitives.dll, aes.dll), then initiating outbound connections with high-entropy payloads. Defender correlates process creation, DLL load, and anomalous encrypted traffic patterns.
- Analytic 'Analytic 0401': Unexpected processes (e.g., bash, python, custom binaries) dynamically loading libcrypto or performing AES/RC4 encryption operations, then initiating outbound sessions with abnormal byte entropy or asymmetric traffic patterns.
- Analytic 'Analytic 0402': Launchd jobs or user processes invoking symmetric crypto APIs from the Security framework and generating outbound connections carrying randomized payloads inconsistent with normal TLS patterns.
- Analytic 'Analytic 0403': ESXi daemons (hostd, vpxa) unexpectedly using symmetric encryption routines for external connections. Defender identifies logs of service traffic with encrypted payloads inconsistent with VMware management baselines.
- Analytic 'Analytic 0404': Flows showing encrypted payloads with high entropy not matching TLS handshake patterns, particularly when occurring on non-standard ports. Defender observes NetFlow/IPFIX byte distribution anomalies or IDS/IPS detecting symmetric encryption patterns without associated key exchange.
T1573.001.procedures: Software 'QuasarRAT': QuasarRAT uses AES with a hardcoded pre-shared key to encrypt network communication. Software 'FRP': FRP can use STCP (Secret TCP) with a preshared key to encrypt services exposed to public networks. Software 'Sliver': Sliver can use AES-GCM-256 to encrypt a session key for C2 message exchange. Software 'Dridex': Dridex has encrypted traffic with RC4. Software 'SMOKEDHAM': SMOKEDHAM has encrypted its C2 traffic with RC4. Software 'InvisiMole': InvisiMole uses variations of a simple XOR encryption routine for C&C communications. Software 'StarProxy': StarProxy has leveraged two 256-byte XOR keys to encrypt and decrypt network packets using a custom algorithm. Software 'SysUpdate': SysUpdate has used DES to encrypt all C2 communications. Software 'Emotet': Emotet is known to use RSA keys for encrypting C2 traffic. Software 'Prikormka': Prikormka encrypts some C2 traffic with the Blowfish cipher. Software '3PARA RAT': 3PARA RAT command and control commands are encrypted within the HTTP C2 channel using the DES algorithm in CBC mode with a key derived from the MD5 hash of the string HYF54&%9&jkMCXuiS. 3PARA RAT will use an 8-byte XOR key derived from the string HYF54&%9&jkMCXuiS if the DES decoding fails Software 'LockBit 3.0': LockBit 3.0 can encrypt C2 communications with AES. Software 'NETEAGLE': NETEAGLE will decrypt resources it downloads with HTTP requests by using RC4 with the key "ScoutEagle." Software 'Machete': Machete has used AES to exfiltrate documents. Software 'Azorult': Azorult can encrypt C2 traffic using XOR. Software 'Bisonal': Bisonal variants reported on in 2014 and 2015 used a simple XOR cipher for C2. Some Bisonal samples encrypt C2 communications with RC4. Software 'Sys10': Sys10 uses an XOR 0x1 loop to encrypt its C2 domain. Software 'Elise': Elise encrypts exfiltrated data with RC4. Software 'Winnti for Windows': Winnti for Windows can XOR encrypt C2 traffic. Software 'Pandora': Pandora has the ability to encrypt communications with D3DES. Software 'BADNEWS': BADNEWS encrypts C2 data with a ROR by 3 and an XOR by 0x23. Software 'NDiskMonitor': NDiskMonitor uses AES to encrypt certain information sent over its C2 channel. Software 'QUIETCANARY': QUIETCANARY can RC4 encrypt C2 communications. Software 'Sardonic': Sardonic has the ability to use an RC4 key to encrypt communications to and from actor-controlled C2 servers. Software 'Attor': Attor has encrypted data symmetrically using a randomly generated Blowfish (OFB) key which is encrypted with a public RSA key. Software 'Lurid': Lurid performs XOR encryption. Software 'StrifeWater': StrifeWater can encrypt C2 traffic using XOR with a hard coded key. Software 'Chaos': Chaos provides a reverse shell connection on 8338/TCP, encrypted via AES. Software 'POWERTON': POWERTON has used AES for encrypting C2 traffic. Software 'Havoc': Havoc can send an AES encrypted check-in request to the C2 server. Software 'SUNBURST': SUNBURST encrypted C2 traffic using a single-byte-XOR cipher. Software 'More_eggs': More_eggs has used an RC4-based encryption method for its C2 communications. Software 'Hikit': Hikit performs XOR encryption. Software 'NETWIRE': NETWIRE can use AES encryption for C2 data transferred. Software 'Helminth': Helminth encrypts data sent to its C2 server over HTTP with RC4. Software 'LODEINFO': LODEINFO can encrypt C2 communication with a hardcoded (NV4HDOeOVyL) Vigenere cipher key. Software 'HotCroissant': HotCroissant has compressed network communications and encrypted them with a custom stream cipher. Software 'VIRTUALPIE': VIRTUALPIE can use a custom RC4 encrypted protocol for C2 communications. Software 'HTTPTroy': HTTPTroy has obfuscated request communications utilizing XOR encryption. Software 'Bonadan': Bonadan can XOR-encrypt C2 communications. Software 'Lucifer': Lucifer can perform a decremental-xor encryption on the initial C2 request before sending it over the wire. Software 'Taidoor': Taidoor uses RC4 to encrypt the message body of HTTP content. Software 'Hi-Zor': Hi-Zor encrypts C2 traffic with a double XOR using two distinct single-byte keys. Software 'FALLCHILL': FALLCHILL encrypts C2 data with RC4 encryption. Software 'Mosquito': Mosquito uses a custom encryption algorithm, which consists of XOR and a stream that is similar to the Blum Blum Shub algorithm. Software 'Dipsind': Dipsind encrypts C2 data with AES256 in ECB mode. Software 'MuddyViper': MuddyViper has the ability to encrypt C2 communication using AES-CBC using the CNG API, the key `0608101047106453101617106423101013101012101083109710108585106969`, and the initialization vector `0`. Software 'CharmPower': CharmPower can send additional modules over C2 encrypted with a simple substitution cipher. Software 'BADCALL': BADCALL encrypts C2 traffic using an XOR/ADD cipher. Software 'WarzoneRAT': WarzoneRAT can encrypt its C2 with RC4 with the password `warzone160\x00`. Software 'HAMMERTOSS': Before being appended to image files, HAMMERTOSS commands are encrypted with a key composed of both a hard-coded value and a string contained on that day's tweet. To decrypt the commands, an investigator would need access to the intended malware sample, the day's tweet, and the image file containing the command. Software 'Mango': Mango can receive XOR-encrypted commands from C2. Software 'LunarWeb': LunarWeb can send AES encrypted C2 commands. Software 'RIFLESPINE': RIFLESPINE can use the AES algorithm to encrypt C2 data. Software 'RustyWater': RustyWater has encrypted encoded data with XOR before sending it to the C2 server. Software 'Uroburos': Uroburos can encrypt the data beneath its http2 or tcp encryption at the session layer with CAST-128, using a different key for incoming and outgoing data. Software 'SideTwist': SideTwist can encrypt C2 communications with a randomly generated key. Software 'OSX_OCEANLOTUS.D': OSX_OCEANLOTUS.D encrypts data sent back to the C2 using AES in CBC mode with a null initialization vector (IV) and a key sent from the server that is padded to 32 bytes. Software 'Emissary': The C2 server response to a beacon sent by a variant of Emissary contains a 36-character GUID value that is used as an encryption key for subsequent network communications. Some variants of Emissary use various XOR operations to encrypt C2 data. Software 'FakeM': The original variant of FakeM encrypts C2 traffic using a custom encryption cipher that uses an XOR key of “YHCRA” and bit rotation between each XOR operation. Some variants of FakeM use RC4 to encrypt C2 traffic. Software 'QakBot': QakBot can RC4 encrypt strings in C2 communication. Software 'Mongall': Mongall has the ability to RC4 encrypt C2 communications. Software 'Cardinal RAT': Cardinal RAT uses a secret key with a series of XOR and addition operations to encrypt C2 traffic. Software 'Sakula': Sakula encodes C2 traffic with single-byte XOR keys. Software 'TAMECAT': TAMECAT has used AES to encrypt C2 traffic. Software 'Volgmer': Volgmer uses a simple XOR cipher to encrypt traffic and files. Software 'httpclient': httpclient encrypts C2 content with XOR using a single byte, 0x12. Software 'LIGHTWIRE': LIGHTWIRE can RC4 encrypt C2 commands. Software 'HiddenWasp': HiddenWasp uses an RC4-like algorithm with an already computed PRGA generated key-stream for network communication. Software 'MoonWind': MoonWind encrypts C2 traffic using RC4 with a static key. Software 'Ninja': Ninja can XOR and AES encrypt C2 messages. Software 'Pikabot': Earlier Pikabot variants use a custom encryption procedure leveraging multiple mechanisms including AES with multiple rounds of Base64 encoding for its command and control communication. Later Pikabot variants eliminate the use of AES and instead use RC4 encryption for transmitted information. Software 'GrimAgent': GrimAgent can use an AES key to encrypt C2 communications. Software 'UPPERCUT': Some versions of UPPERCUT have used the hard-coded string “this is the encrypt key” for Blowfish encryption when communicating with a C2. Later versions have hard-coded keys uniquely for each C2 address. UPPERCUT has also used custom ChaCha20, XOR, and LZO algorithms for C2 communication. Software '4H RAT': 4H RAT obfuscates C2 communication using a 1-byte XOR with the key 0xBE. Software 'Winnti for Linux': Winnti for Linux has used a custom TCP protocol with four-byte XOR for command and control (C2). Software 'PUBLOAD': PUBLOAD has used RC4 encryption in C2 communications. Software 'THINCRUST': THINCRUST can process RSA encryted C2 commands. Software 'TrickBot': TrickBot uses a custom crypter leveraging Microsoft’s CryptoAPI to encrypt C2 traffic.Newer versions of TrickBot have been known to use `bcrypt` to encrypt and digitally sign responses to their C2 server. Software 'Stuxnet': Stuxnet encodes the payload of system information sent to the command and control servers using a one byte 0xFF XOR key. Stuxnet also uses a 31-byte long static byte string to XOR data sent to command and control servers. The servers use a different static key to encrypt replies to the implant. Software 'Torisma': Torisma has encrypted its C2 communications using XOR and VEST-32. Software 'Ebury': Ebury has encrypted C2 traffic using the client IP address, then encoded it as a hexadecimal string. Software 'RDAT': RDAT has used AES ciphertext to encode C2 communications. Software 'XCSSET': XCSSET uses RC4 encryption over TCP to communicate with its C2 server. Software 'Samurai': Samurai can encrypt C2 communications with AES. Software 'ZIPLINE': ZIPLINE can use AES-128-CBC to encrypt data for both upload and download. Software 'Bazar': Bazar can send C2 communications with XOR encryption. Software 'Duqu': The Duqu command and control protocol's data stream can be encrypted with AES-CBC. Software 'Rifdoor': Rifdoor has encrypted command and control (C2) communications with a stream cipher. Software 'Hydraq': Hydraq C2 traffic is encrypted using bitwise NOT and XOR operations. Software 'BBSRAT': BBSRAT uses a custom encryption algorithm on data sent back to the C2 server over HTTP. Software 'ADVSTORESHELL': A variant of ADVSTORESHELL encrypts some C2 with 3DES. Software 'TONESHELL': TONESHELL has used RC4 encryption in C2 communications. TONESHELL variants used a randomly generated variable length (0x20 - 0x200 bytes) rolling XOR key to encrypt and decrypt network packets. Software 'LightNeuron': LightNeuron uses AES to encrypt C2 traffic. Software 'Explosive': Explosive has encrypted communications with the RC4 method. Software 'RTM': RTM encrypts C2 traffic with a custom RC4 variant. Software 'Solar': Solar can XOR encrypt C2 communications. Software 'LookBack': LookBack uses a modified version of RC4 for data transfer. Software 'TAINTEDSCRIBE': TAINTEDSCRIBE uses a Linear Feedback Shift Register (LFSR) algorithm for network encryption. Software 'Kobalos': Kobalos's post-authentication communication channel uses a 32-byte-long password with RC4 for inbound and outbound traffic. Software 'gh0st RAT': gh0st RAT uses RC4 and XOR to encrypt C2 traffic. Software 'CASTLETAP': CASTLETAP can receive a 9-byte XOR encrypted activation string in the payload of an ICMP echo request packet. Software 'Mafalda': Mafalda can encrypt its C2 traffic with RC4. Software 'SombRAT': SombRAT has encrypted its C2 communications with AES. Software 'WellMess': WellMess can encrypt HTTP POST data using RC6 and a dynamically generated AES key encrypted with a hard coded RSA public key. Software 'SystemBC': SystemBC has encrypted its C2 traffic with RC4. Software 'Downdelph': Downdelph uses RC4 to encrypt C2 responses. Software 'BOOKWORM': BOOKWORM has used encryption and compression algorithms to obfuscate the traffic between the system and C2 server, methods observed included RC4, AES, XOR with 0x5a, and LZO. Software 'Comnie': Comnie encrypts command and control communications with RC4. Software 'BendyBear': BendyBear communicates to a C2 server over port 443 using modified RC4 and XOR-encrypted chunks. Software 'PipeMon': PipeMon communications are RC4 encrypted. Software 'HyperStack': HyperStack has used RSA encryption for C2 communications. Software 'Gazer': Gazer uses custom encryption for C2 that uses 3DES. Software 'NGLite': NGLite will use an AES encrypted channel for command and control purposes, in one case using the key WHATswrongwithUu. Software 'NanoCore': NanoCore uses DES to encrypt the C2 traffic. Software 'Derusbi': Derusbi obfuscates C2 traffic with variable 4-byte XOR keys. Software 'SLIGHTPULSE': SLIGHTPULSE can RC4 encrypt all incoming and outgoing C2 messages. Software 'Carbanak': Carbanak encrypts the message body of HTTP traffic with RC2 (in CBC mode). Carbanak also uses XOR with random keys for its communications. Software 'xCaon': xCaon has encrypted data sent to the C2 server using a XOR key. Software 'Okrum': Okrum uses AES to encrypt network traffic. The key can be hardcoded or negotiated with the C2 server in the registration phase. Software 'PoisonIvy': PoisonIvy uses the Camellia cipher to encrypt communications. Software 'Bumblebee': Bumblebee can encrypt C2 requests and responses with RC4 Software 'GreyEnergy': GreyEnergy encrypts communications using AES256. Software 'SodaMaster': SodaMaster can use RC4 to encrypt C2 communications. Software 'RedLeaves': RedLeaves has encrypted C2 traffic with RC4, previously using keys of 88888888 and babybear. Software 'PureCrypter': PureCrypter can use AES to encrypt system information sent to the C2. Software 'FlawedAmmyy': FlawedAmmyy has used SEAL encryption during the initial C2 handshake. Software 'Epic': Epic encrypts commands from the C2 server using a hardcoded key. Software 'Latrodectus': Latrodectus can send RC4 encrypted data over C2 channels. Software 'CallMe': CallMe uses AES to encrypt C2 traffic. Software 'TSCookie': TSCookie has encrypted network communications with RC4. Software 'Komplex': The Komplex C2 channel uses an 11-byte XOR algorithm to hide data. Software 'PlugX': PlugX can use RC4 encryption in C2 communications. Software 'WIREFIRE': WIREFIRE can AES encrypt process output sent from compromised devices to C2. Software 'Cobalt Strike': Cobalt Strike has the ability to use AES-256 symmetric encryption in CBC mode with HMAC-SHA-256 to encrypt task commands and XOR to encrypt shell code and configuration data. Software 'SeaDuke': SeaDuke C2 traffic has been encrypted with RC4 and AES. Software 'RIPTIDE': APT12 has used the RIPTIDE RAT, which communicates over HTTP with a payload encrypted with RC4. Software 'H1N1': H1N1 encrypts C2 traffic using an RC4 key. Software 'KONNI': KONNI has used AES to encrypt C2 traffic. Software 'Daserf': Daserf uses RC4 encryption to obfuscate HTTP traffic. Software 'ZeroT': ZeroT has used RC4 to encrypt C2 traffic. Software 'HiddenFace': HiddenFace can use a randomly selected symmetric encryption algorithm for C2. Software 'Troll Stealer': Troll Stealer encrypts data sent to command and control infrastructure using a combination of RC4 and RSA-4096 algorithms. Software 'FatDuke': FatDuke can AES encrypt C2 communications. Software 'KEYMARBLE': KEYMARBLE uses a customized XOR algorithm to encrypt C2 communications. Software 'Bandook': Bandook has used AES encryption for C2 communication. Software 'IceApple': The IceApple Result Retriever module can AES encrypt C2 responses. Software 'RainyDay': RainyDay can use RC4 to encrypt C2 communications. Software 'PLAINTEE': PLAINTEE encodes C2 beacons using XOR. Software 'FoggyWeb': FoggyWeb has used a dynamic XOR key and custom XOR methodology for C2 communications. Software 'BLINDINGCAN': BLINDINGCAN has encrypted its C2 traffic with RC4. Software 'ChChes': ChChes can encrypt C2 traffic with AES or RC4. Software 'metaMain': metaMain can encrypt the data that it sends and receives from the C2 server using an RC4 encryption algorithm. Software 'UBoatRAT': UBoatRAT encrypts instructions in its C2 network payloads using a simple XOR cipher. Software 'Metamorfo': Metamorfo has encrypted C2 commands with AES-256. Software 'SNUGRIDE': SNUGRIDE encrypts C2 traffic using AES with a static key. Software 'PLEAD': PLEAD has used RC4 encryption to download modules. Software 'CHOPSTICK': CHOPSTICK encrypts C2 communications with RC4. Software 'CORESHELL': CORESHELL C2 messages are encrypted with custom stream ciphers using six-byte or eight-byte keys. Software 'RotaJakiro': RotaJakiro encrypts C2 communication using a combination of AES, XOR, ROTATE encryption, and ZLIB compression. Software 'down_new': down_new has the ability to AES encrypt C2 communications. Software 'Felismus': Some Felismus samples use a custom encryption method for C2 traffic that utilizes AES and multiple keys. Software 'Nebulae': Nebulae can use RC4 and XOR to encrypt C2 communications. Software 'Woody RAT': Woody RAT can use AES-CBC to encrypt data sent to its C2 server. Software 'CosmicDuke': CosmicDuke contains a custom version of the RC4 algorithm that includes a programming error. Software 'PingPull': PingPull can use AES, in cipher block chaining (CBC) mode padded with PKCS5, to encrypt C2 server communications. Group 'Mustang Panda': Mustang Panda has encrypted C2 communications with RC4. Mustang Panda has also leveraged encryption and compression algorithms to obfuscate the traffic between the system and C2 server, methods observed included RC4, AES, XOR with 0x5a, and LZO. Group 'Darkhotel': Darkhotel has used AES-256 and 3DES for C2 communications. Group 'Lazarus Group': Several Lazarus Group malware families encrypt C2 traffic using custom code that uses XOR with an ADD operation and XOR with a SUB operation. Another Lazarus Group malware sample XORs C2 traffic. Other Lazarus Group malware uses Caracachs encryption to encrypt C2 payloads. Lazarus Group has also used AES to encrypt C2 traffic.During Operation Dream Job, Lazarus Group used an AES key to communicate with their C2 server. Group 'ZIRCONIUM': ZIRCONIUM has used AES encrypted communications in C2. Group 'Volt Typhoon': Volt Typhoon has used a version of the Awen web shell that employed AES encryption and decryption for C2 communications. Group 'RedCurl': RedCurl has used AES-128 CBC to encrypt C2 communications. Group 'Stealth Falcon': Stealth Falcon malware encrypts C2 traffic using RC4 with a hard-coded key. Group 'MuddyWater': MuddyWater has used AES to encrypt C2 responses. Group 'Contagious Interview': Contagious Interview has encrypted C2 traffic using RC4. Group 'Higaisa': Higaisa used AES-128 to encrypt C2 traffic. Group 'Inception': Inception has encrypted network communications with AES. Group 'APT33': APT33 has used AES for encryption of command and control traffic. Group 'APT28': APT28 installed a Delphi backdoor that used a custom algorithm for C2 communications. Group 'BRONZE BUTLER': BRONZE BUTLER has used RC4 encryption (for Datper malware) and AES (for xxmm malware) to obfuscate HTTP traffic. BRONZE BUTLER has also used a tool called RarStar that encodes data with a custom XOR algorithm when posting it to a C2 server. Group 'UNC3886': In mid-2024 Mandiant identified custom TINYSHELL-based backdoors deployed on Juniper Networks’ Junos OS routers. Mandiant attributed these backdoors to the China-nexus espionage group UNC3886.During RedPenguin, UNC3886 malware used the RC4 cipher to encrypt outgoing C2 messages. Group 'AppleJeus': During the 3CX Supply Chain Attack, AppleJeus's VEILEDSIGNAL communication module supports three commands to conduct the following actions: send implant data, execute shellcode, and terminate itself. Campaign 'Operation Dream Job': During Operation Dream Job, Lazarus Group used an AES key to communicate with their C2 server. Campaign 'RedPenguin': During RedPenguin, UNC3886 malware used the RC4 cipher to encrypt outgoing C2 messages. Campaign '3CX Supply Chain Attack': During the 3CX Supply Chain Attack, AppleJeus's VEILEDSIGNAL communication module supports three commands to conduct the following actions: send implant data, execute shellcode, and terminate itself. Campaign 'Frankenstein': During Frankenstein, the threat actors communicated with C2 via an encrypted RC4 byte stream and AES-CBC.

T1499.name: Endpoint Denial of Service
T1499.description:
Adversaries may perform Endpoint Denial of Service (DoS) attacks to degrade or block the availability of services to users. Endpoint DoS can be performed by exhausting the system resources those services are hosted on or exploiting the system to cause a persistent crash condition. Example services include websites, email services, DNS, and web-based applications. Adversaries have been observed conducting DoS attacks for political purposes(Citation: FireEye OpPoisonedHandover February 2016) and to support other malicious activities, including distraction(Citation: FSISAC FraudNetDoS September 2012), hacktivism, and extortion.(Citation: Symantec DDoS October 2014)

An Endpoint DoS denies the availability of a service without saturating the network used to provide access to the service. Adversaries can target various layers of the application stack that is hosted on the system used to provide the service. These layers include the Operating Systems (OS), server applications such as web servers, DNS servers, databases, and the (typically web-based) applications that sit on top of them. Attacking each layer requires different techniques that take advantage of bottlenecks that are unique to the respective components. A DoS attack may be generated by a single system or multiple systems spread across the internet, which is commonly referred to as a distributed DoS (DDoS).

To perform DoS attacks against endpoint resources, several aspects apply to multiple methods, including IP address spoofing and botnets.

Adversaries may use the original IP address of an attacking system, or spoof the source IP address to make the attack traffic more difficult to trace back to the attacking system or to enable reflection. This can increase the difficulty defenders have in defending against the attack by reducing or eliminating the effectiveness of filtering by the source address on network defense devices.

Botnets are commonly used to conduct DDoS attacks against networks and services. Large botnets can generate a significant amount of traffic from systems spread across the global internet. Adversaries may have the resources to build out and control their own botnet infrastructure or may rent time on an existing botnet to conduct an attack. In some of the worst cases for DDoS, so many systems are used to generate requests that each one only needs to send out a small amount of traffic to produce enough volume to exhaust the target's resources. In such circumstances, distinguishing DDoS traffic from legitimate clients becomes exceedingly difficult. Botnets have been used in some of the most high-profile DDoS attacks, such as the 2012 series of incidents that targeted major US banks.(Citation: USNYAG IranianBotnet March 2016)

In cases where traffic manipulation is used, there may be points in the global network (such as high traffic gateway routers) where packets can be altered and cause legitimate clients to execute code that directs network packets toward a target in high volume. This type of capability was previously used for the purposes of web censorship where client HTTP traffic was modified to include a reference to JavaScript that generated the DDoS code to overwhelm target web servers.(Citation: ArsTechnica Great Firewall of China)

For attacks attempting to saturate the providing network, see [Network Denial of Service](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1498).
T1499.mitigations: Mitigation 'Filter Network Traffic': Leverage services provided by Content Delivery Networks (CDN) or providers specializing in DoS mitigations to filter traffic upstream from services. Filter boundary traffic by blocking source addresses sourcing the attack, blocking ports that are being targeted, or blocking protocols being used for transport. To defend against SYN floods, enable SYN Cookies.
T1499.detections:
Detection Strategy: Endpoint Resource Saturation and Crash Pattern Detection Across Platforms
- Analytic 'Analytic 0584': Excessive resource exhaustion or service crash induced by processes launched by users or scripts that rapidly consume CPU/memory or attempt malformed service interactions.
- Analytic 'Analytic 0585': Malicious script or binary causes repeated kernel panics, OOM kills, or systemd service restarts targeting services like nginx, httpd, sshd.
- Analytic 'Analytic 0586': Adversary launches high-entropy process or malformed app bundle causing repeated application crashes and system slowdowns.
- Analytic 'Analytic 0587': Instance enters degraded/unhealthy state due to abnormal process load or memory exhaustion, often caused by automation or script-based attacks.
- Analytic 'Analytic 0588': Container orchestrator logs show crashlooping pods, repeated resource exhaustion, or malicious binaries with infinite loops consuming systemd/cgroup limits.
T1499.procedures:
Software 'OnionDuke': OnionDuke has the capability to use a Denial of Service module.
Software 'ZxShell': ZxShell has a feature to perform SYN flood attack on a host.
Group 'Sandworm Team': Sandworm Team temporarily disrupted service to Georgian government, non-government, and private sector websites after compromising a Georgian web hosting provider in 2019.

T1059.007.name: JavaScript
T1059.007.description: Adversaries may abuse various implementations of JavaScript for execution. JavaScript (JS) is a platform-independent scripting language (compiled just-in-time at runtime) commonly associated with scripts in webpages, though JS can be executed in runtime environments outside the browser.(Citation: NodeJS) JScript is the Microsoft implementation of the same scripting standard. JScript is interpreted via the Windows Script engine and thus integrated with many components of Windows such as the [Component Object Model](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1559/001) and Internet Explorer HTML Application (HTA) pages.(Citation: JScrip May 2018)(Citation: Microsoft JScript 2007)(Citation: Microsoft Windows Scripts) JavaScript for Automation (JXA) is a macOS scripting language based on JavaScript, included as part of Apple’s Open Scripting Architecture (OSA), that was introduced in OSX 10.10. Apple’s OSA provides scripting capabilities to control applications, interface with the operating system, and bridge access into the rest of Apple’s internal APIs. As of OSX 10.10, OSA only supports two languages, JXA and [AppleScript](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1059/002). Scripts can be executed via the command line utility osascript, they can be compiled into applications or script files via osacompile, and they can be compiled and executed in memory of other programs by leveraging the OSAKit Framework.(Citation: Apple About Mac Scripting 2016)(Citation: SpecterOps JXA 2020)(Citation: SentinelOne macOS Red Team)(Citation: Red Canary Silver Sparrow Feb2021)(Citation: MDSec macOS JXA and VSCode) Adversaries may abuse various implementations of JavaScript to execute various behaviors. Common uses include hosting malicious scripts on websites as part of a [Drive-by Compromise](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1189) or downloading and executing these script files as secondary payloads. Since these payloads are text-based, it is also very common for adversaries to obfuscate their content as part of [Obfuscated Files or Information](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1027).
T1059.007.mitigations:
Mitigation 'Behavior Prevention on Endpoint': On Windows 10, enable Attack Surface Reduction (ASR) rules to prevent JavaScript scripts from executing potentially malicious downloaded content .
Mitigation 'Execution Prevention': Denylist scripting where appropriate.
Mitigation 'Disable or Remove Feature or Program': Turn off or restrict access to unneeded scripting components.
Mitigation 'Restrict Web-Based Content': Script blocking extensions can help prevent the execution of JavaScript and HTA files that may commonly be used during the exploitation process. For malicious code served up through ads, adblockers can help prevent that code from executing in the first place.
T1059.007.detections:
Detection Strategy: Cross-Platform Detection of JavaScript Execution Abuse
- Analytic 'Analytic 0733': Detects JavaScript execution through WSH (wscript.exe, cscript.exe) or HTA (mshta.exe), particularly when spawned from Office macros, web browsers, or abnormal user paths. Correlates script execution with outbound network activity or system modification.
- Analytic 'Analytic 0734': Detects JavaScript for Automation (JXA) via osascript or compiled scripts using OSAKit APIs. Flags execution involving system modification, inter-process scripting, or browser abuse.
- Analytic 'Analytic 0735': Detects Node.js or JavaScript interpreter execution from web shells, cron jobs, or local users. Correlates execution with reverse shell behavior, file modifications, or abnormal outbound connections.

Software 'FRP': FRP can support the use of a JSON configuration file.
Software 'evilginx2': evilginx2 can inject JavaScript code into HTML content to customize phishing attacks.
Software 'Donut': Donut can generate shellcode outputs that execute via JavaScript or JScript.
Software 'Remcos': Remcos has the ability to execute JavaScript remotely.
Software 'AppleSeed': AppleSeed has the ability to use JavaScript to execute PowerShell.
Software 'Cobalt Strike': The Cobalt Strike System Profiler can use JavaScript to perform reconnaissance actions.
Software 'Metamorfo': Metamorfo includes payloads written in JavaScript.
Software 'BeaverTail': BeaverTail has executed malicious JavaScript code. BeaverTail has also been compiled with the Qt framework to execute in both Windows and macOS.
Software 'NanHaiShu': NanHaiShu executes additional Jscript code on the victim's machine.
Software 'QakBot': The QakBot web inject module can inject Java Script into web banking pages visited by the victim.
Software 'BlackByte Ransomware': BlackByte Ransomware is distributed as a JavaScript launcher file.
Software 'Avaddon': Avaddon has been executed through a malicious JScript downloader.
Software 'StrelaStealer': StrelaStealer has been distributed as a malicious JavaScript object.
Software 'GRIFFON': GRIFFON is written in and executed as JavaScript.
Software 'HexEval Loader': HexEval Loader has executed malicious JavaScript code.
Software 'SocGholish': The SocGholish payload is executed as JavaScript.
Software 'EnvyScout': EnvyScout can write files to disk with JavaScript using a modified version of the open-source tool FileSaver.
Software 'Valak': Valak can execute JavaScript containing configuration data for establishing persistence.
Software 'Latrodectus': Latrodectus has used JavaScript files as part its infection chain during malicious spam email campaigns.
Software 'InvisiMole': InvisiMole can use a JavaScript file as part of its execution chain.
Software 'Astaroth': Astaroth uses JavaScript to perform its core functionalities.
Software 'JSS Loader': JSS Loader can download and execute JavaScript files.
Software 'WARPWIRE': WARPWIRE is a credential harvester written in JavaScript.
Software 'Tsundere Botnet': Tsundere Botnet has the ability to run JavaScript code from the C2 server. Additionally, Tsundere Botnet has used Node.js to execute JavaScript code for the loader component.
Software 'Xbash': Xbash can execute malicious JavaScript payloads on the victim’s machine.
Software 'jRAT': jRAT has been distributed as HTA files with JScript.
Software 'Gootloader': Gootloader can execute a Javascript file for initial infection.
Software 'AshTag': AshTag can use JSON files to deliver payloads and configuration files.
Software 'GlassWorm': GlassWorm has leveraged JavaScript to execute its malicious code to include its hidden Unicode characters using the `eval` call. GlassWorm has also utilized encrypted payloads compiled in JavaScript.
Software 'SpicyOmelette': SpicyOmelette has the ability to execute arbitrary JavaScript code on a compromised host.
Software 'POWERSTATS': POWERSTATS can use JavaScript code for execution.
Software 'Chaes': Chaes has used JavaScript and Node.Js information stealer script that exfiltrates data using the node process.
Software 'Bundlore': Bundlore can execute JavaScript by injecting it into the victim's browser.
Software 'KONNI': KONNI has executed malicious JavaScript code.
Software 'XORIndex Loader': XORIndex Loader has executed malicious JavaScript code.
Software 'KOPILUWAK': KOPILUWAK had used Javascript to perform its core functions.
Software 'Shai-Hulud': Shai-Hulud has used JavaScript to create JSON file output and run scripts using node.js.
Software 'DarkWatchman': DarkWatchman uses JavaScript to perform its core functionalities.
Group 'Turla': Turla has used various JavaScript-based backdoors.
Group 'APT32': APT32 has used JavaScript for drive-by downloads and C2 communications.
Group 'Saint Bear': Saint Bear has delivered malicious Microsoft Office files containing an embedded JavaScript object that would, on execution, download and execute OutSteel and Saint Bot.
Group 'FIN6': FIN6 has used malicious JavaScript to steal payment card data from e-commerce sites.
Group 'Sidewinder': Sidewinder has used JavaScript to drop and execute malware loaders.
Group 'MoustachedBouncer': MoustachedBouncer has used JavaScript to deliver malware hosted on HTML pages.
Group 'APT-C-36': APT-C-36 has used a fileless attack chain composed of three JavaScript code snippets to execute subsequent payloads.
Group 'MuddyWater': MuddyWater has used JavaScript files to execute its POWERSTATS payload.
Group 'Earth Lusca': Earth Lusca has manipulated legitimate websites to inject malicious JavaScript code as part of their watering hole operations.
Group 'TA577': TA577 has used JavaScript to execute additional malicious payloads.
Group 'Winter Vivern': Winter Vivern delivered malicious JavaScript to exploit targets when exploiting Roundcube Webmail servers.
Group 'Silence': Silence has used JS scripts.
Group 'Contagious Interview': Contagious Interview has leveraged JavaScript in the execution of their downloader malware targeting Windows devices using a NodeJS script titled nvidia.js.
Group 'LazyScripter': LazyScripter has used JavaScript in its attacks.
Group 'TA505': TA505 has used JavaScript for code execution.
Group 'FIN7': FIN7 used JavaScript scripts to help perform tasks on the victim's machine.
Group 'Cobalt Group': Cobalt Group has executed JavaScript scriptlets on the victim's machine.
Group 'Higaisa': Higaisa used JavaScript to execute additional files.
Group 'Kimsuky': Kimsuky has used JScript for logging and downloading additional tools. Kimsuky has used TRANSLATEXT, which contained four Javascript files for bypassing defenses, collecting sensitive information and screenshots, and exfiltrating data.
Group 'Indrik Spider': Indrik Spider has used malicious JavaScript files for several components of their attack.
Group 'Molerats': Molerats used various implants, including those built with JS, on target machines.
Group 'Leafminer': Leafminer infected victims using JavaScript code.
Group 'Mustang Panda': Mustang Panda has executed a JavaScript payload utilizing wscript.exe on the endpoint.
Group 'TA578': TA578 has used JavaScript files in malware execution chains.
Group 'Evilnum': Evilnum has used malicious JavaScript files on the victim's machine.
Group 'Star Blizzard': Star Blizzard has used JavaScript to redirect victim traffic from an adversary controlled server to a server hosting the Evilginx phishing framework.
Group 'APT41': During C0017, APT41 deployed JScript web shells on compromised systems.
Campaign 'C0017': During C0017, APT41 deployed JScript web shells on compromised systems.
Campaign 'Water Curupira Pikabot Distribution': Water Curupira Pikabot Distribution initial delivery included obfuscated JavaScript objects stored in password-protected ZIP archives.
Campaign 'Pikabot Distribution February 2024': Pikabot Distribution February 2024 utilized obfuscated JavaScript files for initial Pikabot payload download.
Campaign 'Operation Dust Storm': During Operation Dust Storm, the threat actors used JavaScript code.
Campaign 'C0015': During C0015, the threat actors used a malicious HTA file that contained a mix of encoded HTML and JavaScript/VBScript code.

CVE-2017-5638.title: Apache Struts Remote Code Execution Vulnerability
CVE-2017-5638.description: The Jakarta Multipart parser in Apache Struts 2 2.3.x before 2.3.32 and 2.5.x before 2.5.10.1 has incorrect exception handling and error-message generation during file-upload attempts, which allows remote attackers to execute arbitrary commands via a crafted Content-Type, Content-Disposition, or Content-Length HTTP header, as exploited in the wild in March 2017 with a Content-Type header containing a #cmd= string.
CVE-2017-5638.mitigations: Apply updates per vendor instructions. Not mitigations found in references.

CVE-2006-1547.title: Apache Struts 1 ActionForm Denial-of-Service Vulnerability
CVE-2006-1547.description: ActionForm in Apache Software Foundation (ASF) Struts before 1.2.9 with BeanUtils 1.7 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service via a multipart/form-data encoded form with a parameter name that references the public getMultipartRequestHandler method, which provides further access to elements in the CommonsMultipartRequestHandler implementation and BeanUtils.
CVE-2006-1547.mitigations: Apply updates per vendor instructions. Not mitigations found in references.

CVE-2016-3088.title: Apache ActiveMQ Improper Input Validation Vulnerability
CVE-2016-3088.description: The Fileserver web application in Apache ActiveMQ 5.x before 5.14.0 allows remote attackers to upload and execute arbitrary files via an HTTP PUT followed by an HTTP MOVE request.
CVE-2016-3088.mitigations: Apply updates per vendor instructions. Not mitigations found in references.

mitre_evaluations.explanation: While the attack involves obfuscated payloads, this technique specifically refers to C2 traffic obfuscation, which is not the primary focus of this exploit attempt.
The payload uses encoded characters, but this technique is specific to C2 communication protocols rather than the initial exploit payload delivery.
This technique relates to compiling code after delivery, which is not observed in this Apache Struts RCE attempt.
The alert shows an obfuscated payload being sent to the server, which requires decoding/deobfuscation to execute, making this relevant to the attack chain.
The alert explicitly mentions 'obfuscated exploit attempt' and the log shows a heavily obfuscated payload in the Content-Type header, which is a direct match for this technique.
There is no evidence of data exfiltration in this alert.
This is a server-side exploit attempt, not a spearphishing link attack.
No evidence of symmetric cryptography usage in the provided log.
While the exploit targets a web application, the primary goal is RCE, not a DoS attack.
The payload uses OGNL expressions, which are not strictly JavaScript, though they share some obfuscation characteristics.

cve_evaluations.explanation: The log entry 'JakartaMultiPartRequest' and the specific exploit pattern in the Content-Type header are classic indicators of the Apache Struts CVE-2017-5638 vulnerability.
This is an outdated Struts vulnerability related to DoS, not the RCE attempt observed here.
This vulnerability affects Apache ActiveMQ, not Apache Struts.

rule.description: Suspicious activity: Evasion technique or obfuscated exploit attempt identified.

mitre.description: An external source is sending POST requests containing URL-encoded path traversal sequences to access sensitive system files like /etc/passwd, accompanied by obfuscated data in HTTP headers.
mitre.keywords: path traversal, obfuscated payload, directory traversal

cve.description: The request targets a Citrix ADC appliance by attempting to traverse directories via the /vpn/ path to access system configuration files.

T1001.name: Data Obfuscation
T1001.description: Adversaries may obfuscate command and control traffic to make it more difficult to detect.(Citation: Bitdefender FunnyDream Campaign November 2020) Command and control (C2) communications are hidden (but not necessarily encrypted) in an attempt to make the content more difficult to discover or decipher and to make the communication less conspicuous and hide commands from being seen. This encompasses many methods, such as adding junk data to protocol traffic, using steganography, or impersonating legitimate protocols.
T1001.mitigations: Mitigation 'Network Intrusion Prevention': Network intrusion detection and prevention systems that use network signatures to identify traffic for specific adversary malware can be used to mitigate some obfuscation activity at the network level.
T1001.detections:
Detection Strategy: Detect Obfuscated C2 via Network Traffic Analysis
- Analytic 'Analytic 0144': Detects excessive outbound traffic to remote host over HTTP(S) from uncommon or previously unseen processes.
- Analytic 'Analytic 0145': Identifies custom or previously unseen userland processes initiating high-volume HTTP connections with low response volume.
- Analytic 'Analytic 0146': Flags unexpected user applications initiating long-lived HTTP(S) sessions with irregular traffic patterns.
T1001.procedures:
Software 'evilginx2': evilginx2 can modify the Origin and Referrer fields in HTTPS headers it relays between intended victims and legitimate websites to comply with cross-origin resource sharing (CORS) restrictions.
Software 'StrelaStealer': StrelaStealer encrypts the payload of HTTP POST communications using the same XOR key used for the malware's DLL payload.
Software 'Okrum': Okrum leverages the HTTP protocol for C2 communication, while hiding the actual messages in the Cookie and Set-Cookie headers of the HTTP requests.
Software 'SystemBC': SystemBC has encoded with XOR and encrypted with RC4 its beacon.
Software 'SLOTHFULMEDIA': SLOTHFULMEDIA has hashed a string containing system information prior to exfiltration via POST requests.
Software 'Ninja': Ninja has the ability to modify headers and URL paths to hide malicious traffic in HTTP requests.
Software 'TrailBlazer': TrailBlazer can masquerade its C2 traffic as legitimate Google Notifications HTTP requests.
Software 'FunnyDream': FunnyDream can send compressed and obfuscated packets to C2.
Software 'DarkGate': DarkGate will retrieved encrypted commands from its command and control server for follow-on actions such as cryptocurrency mining.
Software 'FRAMESTING': FRAMESTING can send and receive zlib compressed data within `POST` requests.
Software 'RDAT': RDAT has used encoded data within subdomains as AES ciphertext to communicate from the host to the C2.
Software 'FlawedAmmyy': FlawedAmmyy may obfuscate portions of the initial C2 handshake.
Software 'SideTwist': SideTwist can embed C2 responses in the source code of a fake Flickr webpage.
Group 'Gamaredon Group': Gamaredon Group has used obfuscated VBScripts with randomly generated variable names and concatenated strings.
Campaign 'Operation Wocao': During Operation Wocao, threat actors encrypted IP addresses used for "Agent" proxy hops with RC4.

T1041.name: Exfiltration Over C2 Channel
T1041.description: Adversaries may steal data by exfiltrating it over an existing command and control channel. Stolen data is encoded into the normal communications channel using the same protocol as command and control communications.
T1041.mitigations:
Mitigation 'Network Intrusion Prevention': Network intrusion detection and prevention systems that use network signatures to identify traffic for specific adversary malware can be used to mitigate activity at the network level. Signatures are often for unique indicators within protocols and may be based on the specific obfuscation technique used by a particular adversary or tool, and will likely be different across various malware families and versions. Adversaries will likely change tool command and control signatures over time or construct protocols in such a way to avoid detection by common defensive tools.
Mitigation 'Data Loss Prevention': Data loss prevention can detect and block sensitive data being sent over unencrypted protocols.
T1041.detections:
Detection Strategy: Detection Strategy for Exfiltration Over C2 Channel
- Analytic 'Analytic 0988': Identifies suspicious outbound traffic volume mismatches from processes that typically do not generate network activity, particularly over C2 protocols like HTTPS, DNS, or custom TCP/UDP ports, following file or data access.
- Analytic 'Analytic 0989': Monitors for processes reading sensitive files then immediately initiating unusual outbound connections or bulk transfer sessions over persistent sockets, particularly with encrypted or binary payloads.
- Analytic 'Analytic 0990': Detects unauthorized applications or scripts accessing sensitive data followed by establishing encrypted outbound communication to rare external destinations or with abnormal byte ratios.
- Analytic 'Analytic 0991': Detects VMs sending outbound traffic through non-standard services or to unknown destinations. Exfiltration over reverse shells tunneled via VMkernel or custom payloads routed via hostd/vpxa.
T1041.procedures: Software 'ShimRatReporter': ShimRatReporter sent generated reports to the C2 via HTTP POST requests. Software 'Empire': Empire can send data gathered from a target through the command and control channel. Software 'Pupy': Pupy can send screenshots files, keylogger data, files, and recorded audio back to the C2 server. Software 'SILENTTRINITY': SILENTTRINITY can transfer files from an infected host to the C2 server. Software 'Imminent Monitor': Imminent Monitor has uploaded a file containing debugger logs, network information and system information to the C2. Software 'Sliver': Sliver can exfiltrate files from the victim using the download command. Software 'PcShare': PcShare can upload files and information from a compromised host to its C2 servers. Software 'OilBooster': OilBooster can use an actor-controlled OneDrive account for C2 communication and exfiltration. Software 'MechaFlounder': MechaFlounder has the ability to send the compromised user's account name and hostname within a URL to C2. Software 'LAMEHUG': LAMEHUG can exfiltrate collected system information and documents to C2. Software 'PoetRAT': PoetRAT has exfiltrated data over the C2 channel. Software 'Shark': Shark has the ability to upload files from the compromised host over a DNS or HTTP C2 channel. Software 'Sagerunex': Sagerunex encrypts collected system data then exfiltrates via existing command and control channels. Software 'SLOTHFULMEDIA': SLOTHFULMEDIA has sent system information to a C2 server via HTTP and HTTPS POST requests. Software 'StrelaStealer': StrelaStealer exfiltrates collected email credentials via HTTP POST to command and control servers. Software 'OutSteel': OutSteel can upload files from a compromised host over its C2 channel. Software 'BeaverTail': BeaverTail has exfiltrated data collected from victim devices to C2 servers. Software 'Bandook': Bandook can upload files from a victim's machine over the C2 channel. Software 'BRICKSTORM': BRICKSTORM has uploaded files from the victim system to C2 servers. Software 'HotCroissant': HotCroissant has the ability to download files from the infected host to the command and control (C2) server. Software 'DnsSystem': DnsSystem can exfiltrate collected data to its C2 server. Software 'Machete': Machete's collected data is exfiltrated over the same channel used for C2. Software 'Bumblebee': Bumblebee can send collected data in JSON format to C2. Software 'AppleJeus': AppleJeus has exfiltrated collected host information to a C2 server. Software 'NightClub': NightClub can use SMTP and DNS for file exfiltration and C2. Software 'Line Runner': Line Runner utilizes HTTP to retrieve and exfiltrate information staged using Line Dancer. Software 'WarzoneRAT': WarzoneRAT can send collected victim data to its C2 server. Software 'Torisma': Torisma can send victim data to an actor-controlled C2 server. Software 'SUGARDUMP': SUGARDUMP has sent stolen credentials and other data to its C2 server. Software 'Squirrelwaffle': Squirrelwaffle has exfiltrated victim data using HTTP POST requests to its C2 servers. Software 'FoggyWeb': FoggyWeb can remotely exfiltrate sensitive information from a compromised AD FS server. Software 'Carberp': Carberp has exfiltrated data via HTTP to already established C2 servers. Software 'Misdat': Misdat has uploaded files and data to its C2 servers. Software 'LunarMail': LunarMail can use email image attachments with embedded data for receiving C2 commands and data exfiltration. Software 'Woody RAT': Woody RAT can exfiltrate files from an infected machine to its C2 server. Software 'StrongPity': StrongPity can exfiltrate collected documents through C2 channels. Software 'NETEAGLE': NETEAGLE is capable of reading files over the C2 channel. Software 'LightSpy': To exfiltrate data, LightSpy configures each module to send an obfuscated JSON blob to hardcoded URL endpoints or paths aligned to the module name. Software 'XCSSET': XCSSET retrieves files that match the pattern defined in the INAME_QUERY variable within the user's home directory, such as `*test.txt`, and are below a specific size limit. It then archives the files and exfiltrates the data over its C2 channel. Software 'LightNeuron': LightNeuron exfiltrates data over its email C2 channel. Software 'Grandoreiro': Grandoreiro can send data it retrieves to the C2 server. Software 'TRANSLATEXT': TRANSLATEXT has exfiltrated collected credentials to the C2 server. Software 'Line Dancer': Line Dancer exfiltrates collected data via command and control channels. Software 'TrickBot': TrickBot can send information about the compromised host and upload data to a hardcoded C2 server. Software 'STARWHALE': STARWHALE can exfiltrate collected data to its C2 servers. Software 'BLUELIGHT': BLUELIGHT has exfiltrated data over its C2 channel. Software 'QakBot': QakBot can send stolen information to C2 nodes including passwords, accounts, and emails. Software 'Ursnif': Ursnif has used HTTP POSTs to exfil gathered information. Software 'PowerExchange': PowerExchange can exfiltrate files via its email C2 channel. Software 'LODEINFO': LODEINFO can exfiltrate collected credentials and browser cookies to the C2 server. Software 'AshTag': AshTag has exfiltrated reconnaissance data on targeted systems to C2 servers. Software 'Cannon': Cannon exfiltrates collected data over email via SMTP/S and POP3/S C2 channels. Software 'DarkGate': DarkGate uses existing command and control channels to retrieve captured cryptocurrency wallet credentials. Software 'CharmPower': CharmPower can exfiltrate gathered data to a hardcoded C2 URL via HTTP POST. Software 'Octopus': Octopus has uploaded stolen files and data from a victim's machine over its C2 channel. Software 'S-Type': S-Type has uploaded data and files from a compromised host to its C2 servers. Software 'SVCReady': SVCReady can send collected data in JSON format to its C2 server. Software 'Metamorfo': Metamorfo can send the data it collects to the C2 server. Software 'REvil': REvil can exfiltrate host and malware information to C2 servers. Software 'MobileOrder': MobileOrder exfiltrates data to its C2 server over the same protocol as C2 communications. Software 'Emotet': Emotet has exfiltrated data over its C2 channel. Software 'Amadey': Amadey has sent victim data to its C2 servers. Software 'GoldenSpy': GoldenSpy has exfiltrated host environment information to an external C2 domain via port 9006. Software 'DustySky': DustySky has exfiltrated data to the C2 server. Software 'Zebrocy': Zebrocy has exfiltrated data to the designated C2 server using HTTP POST requests. Software 'Remexi': Remexi performs exfiltration over BITSAdmin, which is also used for the C2 channel. Software 'Doki': Doki has used Ngrok to establish C2 and exfiltrate data. Software 'Kevin': Kevin can send data from the victim host through a DNS C2 channel. Software 'Manjusaka': Manjusaka data exfiltration takes place over HTTP channels. Software 'IceApple': IceApple's Multi File Exfiltrator module can exfiltrate multiple files from a compromised host as an HTTP response over C2. Software 'TajMahal': TajMahal has the ability to send collected files over its C2. Software 'AuTo Stealer': AuTo Stealer can exfiltrate data over actor-controlled C2 servers via HTTP or TCP. Software 'Pikabot': During the initial Pikabot command and control check-in, Pikabot will transmit collected system information encrypted using RC4. Software 'FunnyDream': FunnyDream can execute commands, including gathering user information, and send the results to C2. Software 'Cuckoo Stealer': Cuckoo Stealer can send information about the targeted system to C2 including captured passwords, OS build, hostname, and username. Software 'CHIMNEYSWEEP': CHIMNEYSWEEP can upload collected files to the command-and-control server. Software 'Stuxnet': Stuxnet sends compromised victim information via HTTP. Software 'HOPLIGHT': HOPLIGHT has used its C2 channel to exfiltrate data. Software 'Okrum': Data exfiltration is done by Okrum using the already opened channel with the C2 server. Software 'Astaroth': Astaroth exfiltrates collected information from its r1.log file to the external C2 server. Software 'Crimson': Crimson can exfiltrate stolen information over its C2. Software 'HAWKBALL': HAWKBALL has sent system information and files over the C2 channel. Software 'HTTPTroy': HTTPTroy has exfiltrated encrypted data over the C2 channel using the `up ` command. Software 'SharpDisco': SharpDisco can load a plugin to exfiltrate stolen files to SMB shares also used in C2. Software 'Lokibot': Lokibot has the ability to initiate contact with command and control (C2) to exfiltrate stolen data. Software 'ROKRAT': ROKRAT can send collected files back over same C2 channel. Software 'BoxCaon': BoxCaon uploads files and data from a compromised host over the existing C2 channel. Software 'ThiefQuest': ThiefQuest exfiltrates targeted file extensions in the /Users/ folder to the command and control server via unencrypted HTTP. Network packets contain a string with two pieces of information: a file path and the contents of the file in a base64 encoded string. Software 'Attor': Attor has exfiltrated data over the C2 channel. Software 'Cyclops Blink': Cyclops Blink has the ability to upload exfiltrated files to a C2 server. Software 'EVILNUM': EVILNUM can upload files over the C2 channel from the infected host. Software 'BLINDINGCAN': BLINDINGCAN has sent user and system information to a C2 server via HTTP POST requests. Software 'Latrodectus': Latrodectus can exfiltrate encrypted system information to the C2 server. Software 'SysUpdate': SysUpdate has exfiltrated data over its C2 channel. Software 'MarkiRAT': MarkiRAT can exfiltrate locally stored data via its C2. Software 'ShrinkLocker': ShrinkLocker will exfiltrate victim system information along with the encryption key via an HTTP POST. Software 'MacMa': MacMa exfiltrates data from a supplied path over its C2 channel. Software 'XORIndex Loader': XORIndex Loader has exfiltrated victim data using HTTPS POST requests to its C2 servers. Software 'Valak': Valak has the ability to exfiltrate data over the C2 channel. Software 'PowerShower': PowerShower has used a PowerShell document stealer module to pack and exfiltrate .txt, .pdf, .xls or .doc files smaller than 5MB that were modified during the past two days. Software 'StrifeWater': StrifeWater can send data and files from a compromised host to its C2 server. Software 'Solar': Solar can send staged files to C2 for exfiltration. Software 'Mafalda': Mafalda can send network system data and files to its C2 server. Software 'Mango': Mango can use its HTTP C2 channel for exfiltration. Software 'BACKSPACE': Adversaries can direct BACKSPACE to upload files to the C2 Server. Software 'Lumma Stealer': Lumma Stealer has exfiltrated collected data over existing HTTP and HTTPS C2 channels. Software 'Chrommme': Chrommme can exfiltrate collected data via C2. Software 'Industroyer': Industroyer sends information about hardware profiles and previously-received commands back to the C2 server in a POST-request. Software 'SombRAT': SombRAT has uploaded collected data and files from a compromised host to its C2 server. Software 'FlawedAmmyy': FlawedAmmyy has sent data collected from a compromised host to its C2 servers. T1132.001.name: Standard Encoding
T1132.001.description: Adversaries may encode data with a standard data encoding system to make the content of command and control traffic more difficult to detect. Command and control (C2) information can be encoded using a standard data encoding system that adheres to existing protocol specifications. Common data encoding schemes include ASCII, Unicode, hexadecimal, Base64, and MIME.(Citation: Wikipedia Binary-to-text Encoding)(Citation: Wikipedia Character Encoding) Some data encoding systems may also result in data compression, such as gzip.
T1132.001.mitigations: Mitigation 'Network Intrusion Prevention': Network intrusion detection and prevention systems that use network signatures to identify traffic for specific adversary malware can be used to mitigate activity at the network level. Signatures are often for unique indicators within protocols and may be based on the specific obfuscation technique used by a particular adversary or tool, and will likely be different across various malware families and versions. Adversaries will likely change tool C2 signatures over time or construct protocols in such a way as to avoid detection by common defensive tools.
T1132.001.detections:
Detection Strategy: Behavior-chain detection for T1132.001 Data Encoding: Standard Encoding (Base64/Hex/MIME) across Windows, Linux, macOS, ESXi
- Analytic 'Analytic 0345': Process invokes a standard encoder (e.g., PowerShell -enc, certutil -encode, base64 via .NET/Invoke-Expression) or emits long Base64/hex literals → shortly followed by outbound network egress with high bytes_out:bytes_in ratio or HTTP headers/payloads containing Base64/MIME blocks.
- Analytic 'Analytic 0346': Shell/utility (base64, xxd -p, od, openssl enc -base64, python/perl base64 libraries) encodes data → subsequent outbound connections (curl/wget/bash TCP, socat, python requests) with high asymmetry or Base64/MIME blobs in HTTP/DNS payloads.
- Analytic 'Analytic 0347': Processes use base64/xxd/openssl/python Objective‑C APIs to encode data (seen in EndpointSecurity exec events or Unified Logs) → quick outbound connections with large bytes_out or HTTP POSTs carrying Base64/MIME bodies.
- Analytic 'Analytic 0348': ESXi shell (BusyBox) or VMware utilities (openssl, python if present) used to Base64/hex encode data from datastore or config files → followed by abnormal egress from the host (NSX/flow logs) with asymmetric bytes_out or HTTPS posts to non-management endpoints.

T1598.003.name: Spearphishing Link
T1598.003.description:
Adversaries may send spearphishing messages with a malicious link to elicit sensitive information that can be used during targeting. Spearphishing for information is an attempt to trick targets into divulging information, frequently credentials or other actionable information. Spearphishing for information frequently involves social engineering techniques, such as posing as a source with a reason to collect information (ex: [Establish Accounts](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1585) or [Compromise Accounts](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1586)) and/or sending multiple, seemingly urgent messages.

All forms of spearphishing are electronically delivered social engineering targeted at a specific individual, company, or industry. In this scenario, the malicious emails contain links generally accompanied by social engineering text to coax the user to actively click or copy and paste a URL into a browser.(Citation: TrendMictro Phishing)(Citation: PCMag FakeLogin) The given website may be a clone of a legitimate site (such as an online or corporate login portal) or may closely resemble a legitimate site in appearance and have a URL containing elements from the real site. URLs may also be obfuscated by taking advantage of quirks in the URL schema, such as the acceptance of integer- or hexadecimal-based hostname formats and the automatic discarding of text before an “@” symbol: for example, `hxxp://google.com@1157586937`.(Citation: Mandiant URL Obfuscation 2023)

Adversaries may also embed “tracking pixels,” "web bugs," or "web beacons" within phishing messages to verify the receipt of an email, while also potentially profiling and tracking victim information such as IP address.(Citation: NIST Web Bug)(Citation: Ryte Wiki) These mechanisms often appear as small images (typically one pixel in size) or otherwise obfuscated objects and are typically delivered as HTML code containing a link to a remote server.(Citation: Ryte Wiki)(Citation: IAPP)

Adversaries may also be able to spoof a complete website using what is known as a "browser-in-the-browser" (BitB) attack. By generating a fake browser popup window with an HTML-based address bar that appears to contain a legitimate URL (such as an authentication portal), they may be able to prompt users to enter their credentials while bypassing typical URL verification methods.(Citation: ZScaler BitB 2020)(Citation: Mr. D0x BitB 2022)

Adversaries can use phishing kits such as `EvilProxy` and `Evilginx2` to perform adversary-in-the-middle phishing by proxying the connection between the victim and the legitimate website. On a successful login, the victim is redirected to the legitimate website, while the adversary captures their session cookie (i.e., [Steal Web Session Cookie](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1539)) in addition to their username and password. This may enable the adversary to then bypass MFA via [Web Session Cookie](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1550/004).(Citation: Proofpoint Human Factor)

Adversaries may also send a malicious link in the form of Quick Response (QR) Codes (also known as “quishing”). These links may direct a victim to a credential phishing page.(Citation: QR-campaign-energy-firm) By using a QR code, the URL may not be exposed in the email and may thus go undetected by most automated email security scans.(Citation: qr-phish-agriculture) These QR codes may be scanned by or delivered directly  to a user’s mobile device (i.e., [Phishing](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1660)), which may be less secure in several relevant ways.(Citation: qr-phish-agriculture) For example, mobile users may not be able to notice minor differences between genuine and credential harvesting websites due to mobile’s smaller form factor.

From the fake website, information is gathered in web forms and sent to the adversary. Adversaries may also use information from previous reconnaissance efforts (ex: [Search Open Websites/Domains](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1593) or [Search Victim-Owned Websites](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1594)) to craft persuasive and believable lures.
T1598.003.mitigations:
Mitigation 'User Training': Users can be trained to identify social engineering techniques and spearphishing attempts. Additionally, users may perform visual checks of the domains they visit; however, homographs in ASCII and in IDN domains and URL schema obfuscation may render manual checks difficult. Phishing training and other cybersecurity training may raise awareness to check URLs before visiting the sites.
Mitigation 'Software Configuration': Use anti-spoofing and email authentication mechanisms to filter messages based on validity checks of the sender domain (using SPF) and integrity of messages (using DKIM). Enabling these mechanisms within an organization (through policies such as DMARC) may enable recipients (intra-org and cross domain) to perform similar message filtering and validation. Furthermore, policies may enforce / install browser extensions that protect against IDN and homograph attacks. Browser password managers may also be configured to only populate credential fields when the URL matches that of the original, legitimate site.
T1598.003.detections: Detection Strategy: Detection of Spearphishing Link - Analytic 'Analytic 2010': Monitor for suspicious email activity, such as numerous accounts receiving messages from a single unusual/unknown sender. Filtering based on DKIM+SPF or header analysis can help detect when the email sender is spoofed. Monitor for references to uncategorized or known-bad sites. URL inspection within email (including expanding shortened links and identifying obfuscated URLs) can also help detect links leading to known malicious sites. Furthermore, monitor browser logs for homographs in ASCII and in internationalized domain names abusing different character sets (e.g. Cyrillic vs Latin versions of trusted sites). Monitor network data for uncommon data flows. Processes utilizing the network that do not normally have network communication or have never been seen before are suspicious. Monitor and analyze traffic patterns and packet inspection associated to protocol(s), leveraging SSL/TLS inspection for encrypted traffic, that do not follow the expected protocol standards and traffic flows (e.g extraneous packets that do not belong to established flows, gratuitous or anomalous traffic patterns, anomalous syntax, or structure). Consider correlation with process monitoring and command line to detect anomalous processes execution and command line arguments associated to traffic patterns (e.g. monitor anomalies in use of files that do not normally initiate connections for respective protocol(s)). Furthermore, monitor network traffic for homographs via the use of internationalized domain names abusing different character sets (e.g. Cyrillic vs Latin versions of trusted sites). Also monitor and analyze traffic patterns and packet inspection for indicators of cloned websites. For example, if adversaries use HTTrack to clone websites, Mirrored from (victim URL) may be visible in the HTML section of packets.
T1598.003.procedures:
Software 'AADInternals': AADInternals can send phishing emails containing malicious links designed to collect users’ credentials.
Software 'evilginx2': evilginx2 can generate and display phishing URLs including hidden tracking pixels and can also embed URLs within iframes for browser-in-the-browser phishing.
Software 'SMOKEDHAM': SMOKEDHAM has been delivered via malicious links in phishing emails.
Group 'Sidewinder': Sidewinder has sent e-mails with malicious links to credential harvesting websites.
Group 'Scattered Spider': Scattered Spider has used domains mirroring corporate login portals to socially engineer victims into providing credentials.
Group 'Mustang Panda': Mustang Panda has delivered web bugs to profile their intended targets.
Group 'Sandworm Team': Sandworm Team has crafted spearphishing emails with hyperlinks designed to trick unwitting recipients into revealing their account credentials.
Group 'Silent Librarian': Silent Librarian has used links in e-mails to direct victims to credential harvesting websites designed to appear like the targeted organization's login page.
Group 'ZIRCONIUM': ZIRCONIUM has used web beacons in e-mails to track hits to attacker-controlled URL's.
Group 'APT32': APT32 has used malicious links to direct users to web pages designed to harvest credentials.
Group 'Kimsuky': Kimsuky has used links in e-mail to steal account information including web beacons for target profiling. Kimsuky has also utilized QR codes (also known as Quishing) to direct victims to malicious links through the reliance of a mobile device to scan a code with an embedded malicious URL.
Group 'Magic Hound': Magic Hound has used SMS and email messages with links designed to steal credentials or track victims.
Group 'APT28': APT28 has conducted credential phishing campaigns with links that redirect to credential harvesting sites.
Group 'Star Blizzard': Star Blizzard has sent emails to establish rapport with targets eventually sending messages with links to credential-stealing sites.
Group 'Moonstone Sleet': Moonstone Sleet used spearphishing messages containing items such as tracking pixels to determine if users interacted with malicious messages.
Group 'CURIUM': CURIUM used malicious links to adversary-controlled resources for credential harvesting.
Group 'Dragonfly': Dragonfly has used spearphishing with PDF attachments containing malicious links that redirected to credential harvesting websites.
Group 'Patchwork': Patchwork has used embedded image tags (known as web bugs) with unique, per-recipient tracking links in their emails for the purpose of identifying which recipients opened messages.

T1071.001.name: Web Protocols
T1071.001.description:
Adversaries may communicate using application layer protocols associated with web traffic to avoid detection/network filtering by blending in with existing traffic. Commands to the remote system, and often the results of those commands, will be embedded within the protocol traffic between the client and server. 

Protocols such as HTTP/S(Citation: CrowdStrike Putter Panda) and WebSocket(Citation: Brazking-Websockets) that carry web traffic may be very common in environments. HTTP/S packets have many fields and headers in which data can be concealed. An adversary may abuse these protocols to communicate with systems under their control within a victim network while also mimicking normal, expected traffic.
T1071.001.mitigations:
Mitigation 'Network Intrusion Prevention': Network intrusion detection and prevention systems that use network signatures to identify traffic for specific adversary malware can be used to mitigate activity at the network level.
Mitigation 'Filter Network Traffic': Restrict and monitor outbound web traffic (HTTP/HTTPS) from critical servers to only approved destinations. Limiting the ability to initiate outbound HTTP/HTTPS connections, especially from public-facing servers, can prevent attackers from using tools like curl or wget to communicate with external C2 servers or download malicious payloads.
T1071.001.detections:
Detection Strategy: Detection of Web Protocol-Based C2 Over HTTP, HTTPS, or WebSockets
- Analytic 'Analytic 0075': Detects unexpected or high-volume HTTP/S/WebSocket communication from suspicious processes (e.g., PowerShell, rundll32) using uncommon user agents or mimicking browser traffic to unusual domains or IPs.
- Analytic 'Analytic 0076': Detects curl, wget, Python requests, or custom HTTP clients communicating over non-standard ports, with repetitive or beacon-like patterns or POST-heavy behavior to rare domains.
- Analytic 'Analytic 0077': Detects applications such as Automator, AppleScript, or LaunchDaemons invoking HTTP/S traffic to non-standard domains or using suspicious headers (e.g., Base64 in URIs or cookie fields).
- Analytic 'Analytic 0078': Detects HTTP or HTTPS communication initiated by shell-based scripts or management daemons, especially those reaching public IPs over ports 80/443 using embedded curl or wget.
- Analytic 'Analytic 0079': Detects Web protocol misuse such as encoded HTTP headers, WebSocket upgrade requests with abnormal payloads, or TLS handshake anomalies suggesting embedded C2 channels.

T1102.001.name: Dead Drop Resolver
T1102.001.description:
Adversaries may use an existing, legitimate external Web service to host information that points to additional command and control (C2) infrastructure. Adversaries may post content, known as a dead drop resolver, on Web services with embedded (and often obfuscated/encoded) domains or IP addresses. Once infected, victims will reach out to and be redirected by these resolvers.

Popular websites and social media acting as a mechanism for C2 may give a significant amount of cover due to the likelihood that hosts within a network are already communicating with them prior to a compromise. Using common services, such as those offered by Google or Twitter, makes it easier for adversaries to hide in expected noise. Web service providers commonly use SSL/TLS encryption, giving adversaries an added level of protection.

Use of a dead drop resolver may also protect back-end C2 infrastructure from discovery through malware binary analysis while also enabling operational resiliency (since this infrastructure may be dynamically changed).
T1102.001.mitigations:
Mitigation 'Restrict Web-Based Content': Web proxies can be used to enforce external network communication policy that prevents use of unauthorized external services.
Mitigation 'Network Intrusion Prevention': Network intrusion detection and prevention systems that use network signatures to identify traffic for specific adversary malware can be used to mitigate activity at the network level.
T1102.001.detections:
Detection Strategy: Detection Strategy for Web Service: Dead Drop Resolver
- Analytic 'Analytic 0158': Detection of a process or script that accesses a common web service to retrieve content containing obfuscated indicators of a secondary C2 server (dead drop resolver behavior).
- Analytic 'Analytic 0159': Detection of a process or script that accesses a common web service to retrieve content containing obfuscated indicators of a secondary C2 server (dead drop resolver behavior).
- Analytic 'Analytic 0160': Detection of a process or script that accesses a common web service to retrieve content containing obfuscated indicators of a secondary C2 server (dead drop resolver behavior).
- Analytic 'Analytic 0161': Detection of a process or script that accesses a common web service to retrieve content containing obfuscated indicators of a secondary C2 server (dead drop resolver behavior).
T1102.001.procedures:
Software 'Astaroth': Astaroth can store C2 information on cloud hosting services such as AWS and CloudFlare and websites like YouTube and Facebook.
Software 'BADNEWS': BADNEWS collects C2 information via a dead drop resolver.
Software 'KEYPLUG': The KEYPLUG Windows variant has retrieved C2 addresses from encoded data in posts on tech community forums.
Software 'MiniDuke': Some MiniDuke components use Twitter to initially obtain the address of a C2 server or as a backup if no hard-coded C2 server responds.
Software 'Grandoreiro': Grandoreiro can obtain C2 information from Google Docs.
Software 'Metamorfo': Metamorfo has used YouTube to store and hide C&C server domains.
Software 'PlugX': PlugX uses Pastebin to store C2 addresses.
Software 'GlassWorm': GlassWorm has leveraged blockchain-based C2 infrastructure to include Solana blockchain that contains additional C2 details within the memo field. GlassWorm has also leveraged Google Calendar to host encoded data.
Software 'CharmPower': CharmPower can retrieve C2 domain information from actor-controlled S3 buckets.
Software 'TRANSLATEXT': TRANSLATEXT has used a dead drop resolver to retrieve configurations and commands from a public blog site.
Software 'Tsundere Botnet': Tsundere Botnet has obtained the C2 address from Ethereum blockchain nodes.
Software 'BLACKCOFFEE': BLACKCOFFEE uses Microsoft’s TechNet Web portal to obtain a dead drop resolver containing an encoded tag with the IP address of a command and control server.
Software 'RTM': RTM has used an RSS feed on Livejournal to update a list of encrypted C2 server names. RTM has also hidden Pony C2 server IP addresses within transactions on the Bitcoin and Namecoin blockchain.
Software 'PolyglotDuke': PolyglotDuke can use Twitter, Reddit, Imgur and other websites to get a C2 URL.
Software 'Javali': Javali can read C2 information from Google Documents and YouTube.
Software 'MOPSLED': MOPSLED has the ability to retrieve a C2 address from a dead drop URL.
Software 'Xbash': Xbash can obtain a webpage hosted on Pastebin to update its C2 domain list.
Group 'Rocke': Rocke has used Pastebin to check the version of beaconing malware and redirect to another Pastebin hosting updated malware.
